studyMetadata.title: Travelling with Jonah
studyMetadata.subtitle: A Double Journey
studyMetadata.description:
Jonah is an unusual book. If you are familiar with the Bible, it is probably one you feel fairly at home with — one you know quite well (or parts of quite well). The part about ’the whale’ is usually familiar territory. But there is a lot more to the book than just fish.
’Do not be like Jonah.’ — the message in a sentence
The story is not as straightforward as it looks at first glance. There are surprises, reversals and irony. Our hero does not look quite so heroic by the end — and some of the ’villains’ almost turn out to be heroes. We will journey through Jonah twice: forwards from chapter 1 to 4, then backwards from 4 to 1.
studyMetadata.author: Rick Creighton

theme.theme-annotation: Travelling with Jonah — deep sea blues and storm-grey tones, warmed by desert ochres and Nineveh earth, evoking the journey from rebellion to compassion, from the depths of the sea to the burning east wind.

elements.text: Most people feel fairly at home with Jonah — the whale is classic Sunday school material. But the book is not as straightforward as it looks at first glance. There are surprises, reversals, and irony. Our so-called hero does not look quite so heroic by the end — and some of the apparent villains almost turn out to be heroes. Before we get to the surprises, we need to understand the world Jonah lived in.

elements.body:
Nineveh was one of the main cities of Assyria — the superpower of Jonah’s day. Assyria’s empire was enormous, and its reputation was ferocious. They enjoyed inflicting war crimes on defeated enemies: survivors impaled on stakes, heads displayed as trophies. The relationship between Israel and Assyria was one of deep mutual hatred — and it was Jonah’s job to preach to them.

Originally Israel was one nation, ruled by King David. After Solomon it split: the southern half was called Judah; the larger northern half kept the name Israel. Jonah lived in the northern kingdom. (1 Kings 12 tells the story of the division; 2 Kings 14:25 tells us Jonah was based in the north.)

elements.question: Read the whole of chapter 1 before you begin…
elements.answer:
1The word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai: 2’Go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me.’ 3But Jonah ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish. He went down to Joppa, where he found a ship bound for that port. After paying the fare, he went aboard and sailed for Tarshish to flee from the Lord.

4Then the Lord sent a great wind on the sea, and such a violent storm arose that the ship threatened to break up. 5All the sailors were afraid and each cried out to his own god. And they threw the cargo into the sea to lighten the ship.

But Jonah had gone below deck, where he lay down and fell into a deep sleep. 6The captain went to him and said, ’How can you sleep? Get up and call on your god! Maybe he will take notice of us so that we will not perish.’

7Then the sailors said to each other, ’Come, let us cast lots to find out who is responsible for this calamity.’ They cast lots and the lot fell on Jonah. 8So they asked him, ’Tell us, who is responsible for making all this trouble for us? What kind of work do you do? Where do you come from? What is your country? From what people are you?’

9He answered, ’I am a Hebrew and I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.’

10This terrified them and they asked, ’What have you done?’ (They knew he was running away from the Lord, because he had already told them so.)

11The sea was getting rougher and rougher. So they asked him, ’What should we do to you to make the sea calm down for us?’

12’Pick me up and throw me into the sea,‘ he replied, ’and it will become calm. I know that it is my fault that this great storm has come upon you.’

13Instead, the men did their best to row back to land. But they could not, for the sea grew even wilder than before. 14Then they cried out to the Lord, ’Please, Lord, do not let us die for taking this man’s life. Do not hold us accountable for killing an innocent man, for you, Lord, have done as you pleased.’ 15Then they took Jonah and threw him overboard, and the raging sea grew calm. 16At this the men greatly feared the Lord, and they offered a sacrifice to the Lord and made vows to him.

17Now the Lord provided a huge fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.

elements.text: As you read, notice who does what. Pay attention to the contrasts between Jonah and the sailors — and watch what God is doing throughout.

elements.question: What impression do you get of Jonah from your first reading of chapter 1? And what impression do you get of the sailors?

elements.text: Scene 1 — Call and Response (1:1–3)

elements.passageText: 1The word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai: 2’Go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me.’ 3But Jonah ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish. He went down to Joppa, where he found a ship bound for that port. After paying the fare, he went aboard and sailed for Tarshish to flee from the Lord.

elements.question: God’s call to Jonah is simple and direct (vv1–2). What exactly does God ask him to do, and why?
elements.sampleAnswer: God tells Jonah to go to Nineveh — described as a ‘great city’ — and preach against it. The reason is explicit: ‘its wickedness has come up before me.’ God is not unaware of what is happening in Nineveh. He sees it, he cares about it, and he is sending his prophet to speak into it. The call is straightforward: no complicated strategy, no softening of the message. Go. Preach. That is it. What makes it extraordinary is the context: Nineveh is the capital of Israel’s most terrifying enemy. God is not asking Jonah to preach to his own people but to the people who most threaten and brutalise them.
elements.questionHint: Notice that God gives Jonah both a destination and a reason. What does the reason tell us about God’s relationship to the wider world beyond Israel?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God describes Nineveh’s wickedness as having ‘come up before’ him. What does that phrase suggest about how God sees and responds to evil in the world?

elements.question: Jonah’s response in v3 is abrupt: he runs. Look at the geography. Where was he told to go, and where does he actually head? What does this tell us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Nineveh lay to the north-east of Israel. Jonah heads south-west — to Joppa on the coast, where he catches a ship for Tarshish, most likely modern-day Spain. He is not just refusing; he is going as far as possible in exactly the opposite direction. The text hammers the point home by using the phrase ‘flee from the Lord’ twice in a single verse. Jonah is not drifting or hesitating; he is making a deliberate, determined run for it. At this point the text does not tell us why — we have to wait until chapter 4 for that. For now, the natural guess is fear: preaching judgment to 120,000 brutal Assyrians is about as daunting a mission as it is possible to imagine.
elements.questionHint: The text uses the phrase ‘to flee from the Lord’ twice. What does the repetition suggest about Jonah’s state of mind?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Do you think Jonah actually believed he could flee from God? What might it tell us about how we think when we are in rebellion — even when we know better?

elements.text: Scene 2 — Storm and Response (1:4–10)

elements.passageText: 4Then the Lord sent a great wind on the sea, and such a violent storm arose that the ship threatened to break up. 5All the sailors were afraid and each cried out to his own god. And they threw the cargo into the sea to lighten the ship. But Jonah had gone below deck, where he lay down and fell into a deep sleep. 6The captain went to him and said, ’How can you sleep? Get up and call on your god! Maybe he will take notice of us so that we will not perish.’ 7Then the sailors said to each other, ’Come, let us cast lots to find out who is responsible for this calamity.’ They cast lots and the lot fell on Jonah. 8So they asked him, ’Tell us, who is responsible for making all this trouble for us? What kind of work do you do? Where do you come from? What is your country? From what people are you?’ 9He answered, ’I am a Hebrew and I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.’ 10This terrified them and they asked, ’What have you done?’ (They knew he was running away from the Lord, because he had already told them so.)

elements.question: When the storm hits, how do the sailors respond (vv4–6)? How does Jonah respond? What is striking about the contrast?
elements.sampleAnswer: The sailors respond exactly as you would expect: they are terrified, they cry out to their gods, and they do everything practically possible to save the ship — throwing the cargo overboard to lighten the load. They are active, urgent, and afraid. Jonah, by contrast, is asleep. He has gone below deck and is fast asleep through a storm so violent it threatens to break the ship apart. The contrast is almost comic in its absurdity — except that it is not funny at all. The pagan sailors are doing everything right while God’s own prophet is doing nothing. The irony deepens when the captain — a pagan — has to go and wake Jonah and tell him to pray to his god. The Lord’s prophet needs a pagan to remind him to pray.
elements.questionHint: The captain’s words to Jonah (‘How can you sleep? Get up and call on your god!’) are dripping with irony. What does the fact that a pagan has to say this to God’s prophet tell us about the state Jonah is in?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Jonah sleeps through the storm. Is this simply exhaustion, or does it suggest something deeper about his spiritual state? What does it look like when someone is so deep in rebellion that they cannot even engage with the crisis around them?

elements.body:
We begin to get ourselves in a mess, but we try to pretend it is not happening — or we think that if we just ignore it, it will go away. That is what Jonah did by sleeping through the storm. But like most messes, it did not go away; it only got worse. Jonah may have been in denial, but the storm was not.

Ignoring the messes we make is not the godly way to respond. The godly response is to own up — primarily to God, and often to other people too. One lesson we ought to learn from Jonah is to face up to our failings rather than pretend they are not there.
Have I made any ‘messes’ that I need to face up to?

elements.question: When the lot falls on Jonah and the sailors question him (vv8–9), what does he say about himself and about God? Why does this make his behaviour all the more remarkable?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s confession is theologically impeccable: ‘I am a Hebrew and I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.’ He knows exactly who God is — the Creator of the very sea that is now threatening to kill them all. This is precisely what makes his behaviour so extraordinary and so damning. He is not running from a god he does not know or understand. He is running from the God of heaven who made the sea — and he is doing it on a boat, on that sea. The sailors are terrified when they hear this, and their question — ‘What have you done?!’ — is the natural response. Even pagans can see the absurdity of trying to flee from the Creator of the ocean by sailing across it. Jonah apparently could not.
elements.questionHint: Jonah says he worships ‘the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.’ Given that he is on a ship in a storm, why is this confession particularly ironic?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: There is a difference between knowing the truth about God and living by it. Jonah clearly knows who God is. What does his behaviour tell us about that gap? Where do you notice the same gap in your own life?

elements.body: Jonah might hold up an uncomfortable mirror to us here. If we openly disobey God, that will show through in how we act — we are not so keen to think about him, pray to him, or talk about him much with others. It does not just damage us: it robs the people around us of a chance to hear about the Lord who saves.

elements.text: Scene 3 — Crunch Time (1:11–17)

elements.passageText: 11The sea was getting rougher and rougher. So they asked him, ’What should we do to you to make the sea calm down for us?’ 12’Pick me up and throw me into the sea,‘ he replied, ’and it will become calm. I know that it is my fault that this great storm has come upon you.’ 13Instead, the men did their best to row back to land. But they could not, for the sea grew even wilder than before. 14Then they cried out to the Lord, ’Please, Lord, do not let us die for taking this man’s life. Do not hold us accountable for killing an innocent man, for you, Lord, have done as you pleased.’ 15Then they took Jonah and threw him overboard, and the raging sea grew calm. 16At this the men greatly feared the Lord, and they offered a sacrifice to the Lord and made vows to him.

elements.question: When the sailors ask Jonah what to do, he tells them to throw him overboard (v12). What does he understand about the situation? What is notable about the sailors’ response in v13?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah understands clearly: the storm is his fault, it is God’s doing, and the only way to save the others is to remove himself from the equation. That much is to his credit — he does not try to hide or deflect responsibility. He owns it. But notably, he does not pray, does not repent, does not turn around and go to Nineveh. He would rather go overboard than go to Nineveh. His solution is to exit the situation, not to change course. The sailors’ response is remarkable: despite knowing Jonah is the cause of all their trouble, they refuse to throw him over. They row as hard as they can to try to get him to land safely. These rough, pagan sailors are doing everything they can to save the life of the man whose disobedience nearly killed them all — while Jonah himself is apparently willing to drown.
elements.questionHint: Jonah takes responsibility for the storm — but notice what he does not do. What options were available to him that he did not take?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Is there a difference between taking responsibility for a mess and genuinely repenting of it? Which is Jonah doing here — and what does the distinction look like in your own experience?

elements.question: When the sailors finally throw Jonah overboard and the sea goes calm, how do they respond (v16)? What is striking about this — given everything that has happened?
elements.sampleAnswer: The sailors ‘greatly feared the Lord’ and offered sacrifice and made vows to him. In other words, they become worshippers of Yahweh — the very God that Jonah was running away from. This is one of the great ironies of the chapter: Jonah’s flight from God, his disobedience, his near-drowning — all of it has ended up bringing a group of pagan sailors to faith in the Lord. God has used Jonah’s failure as the instrument of these men’s conversion. Jonah made no real effort to point them toward God — he was an absolute failure as a prophet — and yet God still worked through the chaos to bring these sailors to himself. That is a striking picture of God’s sovereignty: he can bring good out of even our most spectacular failures.
elements.questionHint: The sailors end up fearing the Lord and making vows to him. Who converted them? What does this tell us about whose work salvation ultimately is?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The sailors respond to God more faithfully than the prophet of God does throughout this chapter. What does that say to us about where genuine faith can be found — and about our assumptions regarding who is ‘inside’ and ‘outside’?

elements.body:
There is a striking string of reversals between the prophet Jonah and the pagan sailors:

• The pagan captain has to tell Jonah the prophet to pray.
• The sailors work desperately to save Jonah’s life when he has put theirs in danger — while he sleeps.
• The sailors understand the seriousness of Jonah’s disobedience far better than he does. ‘What have you done?!’ they ask. Jonah has no answer.
• The sailors begin to worship the true God while Jonah is still running away from him.

The only good responses in this whole chapter come from the sailors. Of Jonah we hear no prayer, no worship, no apology, no repentance — nothing.
Are you running away from anything that God wants you to do?

elements.text: God in Control (1:17)

elements.passageText: 17Now the Lord provided a huge fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.

elements.question: Look back over the whole of chapter 1. Make a list of everything God controls or ‘provides.’ What picture of God emerges?
elements.sampleAnswer: God sends the storm (v4). God’s purpose is behind the casting of the lot that identifies Jonah (v7). God provides the great fish to swallow Jonah (v17). Even the calming of the sea when Jonah goes overboard is God’s doing (v15). Throughout the chapter, God is the one initiating, directing, and controlling events. The picture that emerges is of God’s total sovereignty — nothing happens outside his knowledge or beyond his reach. He is not thrown by Jonah’s rebellion; he uses it. He is not defeated by the storm; he sent it. He is not scrambling to recover a situation that got away from him; he is guiding the whole story toward his purpose. This is enormously reassuring: the technical word is sovereignty, and it means that in any situation — even the messiest — God has not lost the plot.
elements.questionHint: The text says God ‘sent’ the storm and ‘provided’ the fish. What do those active verbs tell us about God’s relationship to the events of the chapter?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God’s sovereignty in this chapter means he can bring good out of Jonah’s complete failure. Does that make Jonah’s disobedience less serious? How do we hold together God’s sovereignty and human responsibility?

elements.question: Jonah may have given up — perhaps he is even willing to drown rather than go to Nineveh. But v17 says God provided a fish. What does this tell us about how God responds to people who have failed him?
elements.sampleAnswer: God has not given up on Jonah. He has not said, ‘You have failed — you are finished as a prophet.’ Instead he provides a fish — an unlikely, undignified, smelly rescue — to preserve Jonah’s life and give him time to reflect, repent, and learn. The fish is an act of grace, not punishment. Jonah deserved to drown; God saved him anyway. This is one of the defining characteristics of God throughout the book: he does not abandon people who have failed him. He pursues them. He provides for them even when they have walked away. The fish is not comfortable — but it is gracious. That is what grace often looks like: not what we wanted, but exactly what we needed.
elements.questionHint: The fish is often thought of as the dramatic centrepiece of Jonah — but it actually appears in only one verse in chapter 1. What is it doing in the story? Is it a punishment or something else?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Can you think of a time when God’s grace to you came in an uncomfortable or unexpected form — something that did not feel like a gift at the time? What does Jonah’s fish teach us about how to recognise grace?

elements.text: Reflection

elements.passageText: 1The word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai: 2’Go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me.’ 3But Jonah ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish. He went down to Joppa, where he found a ship bound for that port. After paying the fare, he went aboard and sailed for Tarshish to flee from the Lord.

elements.question: Jonah ran away from God’s call — but he could not outrun God. Is there something you know God is calling you to do that you have been avoiding? What is making it feel too daunting?
elements.sampleAnswer: This question is personal and the answer will be different for everyone. What Jonah’s story shows is that avoidance does not make the call go away — it just means we end up in a far bigger mess than if we had obeyed in the first place. The thing we are avoiding is usually daunting for a reason: it may cost us something, expose us to hostility, take us out of our comfort zone, or require us to trust God in a situation where we would prefer to stay in control. The question worth sitting with is not ‘is this hard?’ but ‘is this what God is asking of me?’ Because if it is, then the God who controlled the storm, the lot, and the fish is the same God who will be in control of whatever we step into when we obey.
elements.questionHint: Jonah’s call was to a specific place, to specific people, with a specific message. Is there a specific thing — not just a vague sense — that you have been putting off?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: What would it look like to take a step toward the thing you have been avoiding — this week, specifically? What is the smallest concrete move in the right direction?

elements.passageText: 4Then the Lord sent a great wind on the sea, and such a violent storm arose that the ship threatened to break up. 5All the sailors were afraid and each cried out to his own god. And they threw the cargo into the sea to lighten the ship. But Jonah had gone below deck, where he lay down and fell into a deep sleep. 6The captain went to him and said, ’How can you sleep? Get up and call on your god! Maybe he will take notice of us so that we will not perish.’

elements.question: The ‘Ostrich Trick’ — burying our heads and hoping a problem goes away — is something most of us recognise. Is there a mess in your life right now that you have been sleeping through rather than facing up to? What would owning up — to God, or to someone else — look like?
elements.sampleAnswer: The Ostrich Trick feels safer in the moment because awareness of a problem means having to deal with it. Jonah’s sleep is a picture of someone who has decided, at some level, that not knowing is better than knowing — even when the storm is clearly raging. The godly response is not to be a perfect person who never makes messes; it is to be someone who faces them honestly rather than pretending they are not there. Owning up to God means bringing the specific thing before him in prayer — not vague confession but naming it clearly. Owning up to another person means finding someone you trust and being honest with them about what is going on. Both are uncomfortable. Both are far better than staying asleep while the storm gets worse.
elements.questionHint: Is the mess something only God needs to know about, or is there also a person — someone you have wronged, or someone who could help — who needs to hear from you?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Why is owning up hard? What do we fear will happen if we admit we have made a mess — to God, or to another person? How does the picture of God in this chapter speak to those fears?

elements.passageText: …15Then they took Jonah and threw him overboard, and the raging sea grew calm. 16At this the men greatly feared the Lord, and they offered a sacrifice to the Lord and made vows to him. 17Now the Lord provided a huge fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.

elements.question: God used Jonah’s spectacular failure to bring the sailors to faith in himself. He then saved Jonah from drowning with an unlikely fish. What does God’s sovereignty in this chapter — his ability to bring good out of even our worst failures — mean for you in your current situation?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s sovereignty in chapter 1 is not a theological abstraction — it is a pastoral reality. It means that no situation is so broken, no failure so complete, that God cannot work in it and through it. He used Jonah’s disobedience to save the sailors. He used the storm to get Jonah’s attention. He used a fish to preserve Jonah’s life. None of those outcomes required Jonah to be at his best. God worked around Jonah’s worst. For us, this means that whatever mess we are in — whether of our own making or not — God has not lost the plot. That does not mean everything will be fine immediately, or that consequences will disappear. But it does mean that even in the middle of a storm, ‘the mess will not have the last word.’ God is still in control. And that is worth calling out to him about.
elements.questionHint: Is there a situation in your life right now that feels out of control or beyond recovery? What would it mean to trust that the God who controlled Jonah’s storm is also present in your situation?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The chapter ends with Jonah inside a fish — alive, but not yet where he needs to be. God’s rescue is real but incomplete. How does that image speak to your experience of God’s help — which is often real but rarely instant or comfortable?

elements.text: Have you ever made a mistake and ended up in an enormous mess? Nearly everyone has, so we can probably all identify with where Jonah finds himself — to some extent. Very few people will ever have been in quite the mess that Jonah is in. He has barely escaped drowning, and only because a sea monster eats him first. Jonah is stuck inside a whale. Chapter 2 is the prayer he prays from there.

elements.question: Read the whole of chapter 2 before you begin…
elements.answer:
1From inside the fish Jonah prayed to the Lord his God. 2He said:

"In my distress I called to the Lord,
and he answered me.
From the depths of the grave I called for help,
and you listened to my cry.
3You hurled me into the deep,
into the very heart of the seas,
and the currents swirled about me;
all your waves and breakers
swept over me.
4I said, ‘I have been banished
from your sight;
yet I will look again
towards your holy temple.’
5The engulfing waters threatened me,
the deep surrounded me;
seaweed was wrapped around my head.
6To the roots of the mountains I sank down;
the earth beneath barred me in for ever.
But you, Lord my God,
brought my life up from the pit.
7When my life was ebbing away,
I remembered you, Lord,
and my prayer rose to you,
to your holy temple.
8Those who cling to worthless idols
turn away from God’s love for them.
9But I, with shouts of grateful praise,
will sacrifice to you.
What I have vowed I will make good.
I will say, ‘Salvation comes from the Lord.’"

10And the Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

elements.text: As you read Jonah’s prayer, notice the movement: where he starts, what he remembers, what he resolves, and how the chapter ends.

elements.question: What is the overall tone or mood of Jonah’s prayer? Does it surprise you, given what he was like in chapter 1?

elements.text: Sorrow Recognises the Problem (2:1–6)

elements.passageText: "In my distress I called to the Lord, and he answered me. From the depths of the grave I called for help, and you listened to my cry. 3You hurled me into the deep, into the very heart of the seas, and the currents swirled about me; all your waves and breakers swept over me. 4I said, ‘I have been banished from your sight; yet I will look again towards your holy temple.’ 5The engulfing waters threatened me, the deep surrounded me; seaweed was wrapped around my head. 6To the roots of the mountains I sank down; the earth beneath barred me in for ever. But you, Lord my God, brought my life up from the pit."

elements.question: In vv3–6, Jonah describes his situation in vivid, physical detail. What images does he use? What do they convey about how desperate his position is?
elements.sampleAnswer: The images pile up: hurled into the deep, currents swirling, waves and breakers sweeping over him, seaweed wrapped around his head, sinking to the roots of the mountains, the earth barring him in forever. Each image takes him deeper — further from the surface, further from air, further from any possibility of self-rescue. The phrase ‘the earth beneath barred me in for ever’ is the low point: this is the language of a tomb, of permanent death. What the imagery conveys is not just physical danger but utter helplessness. There is nothing Jonah can do. He cannot swim up from the roots of the mountains. He cannot unwrap the seaweed himself. He cannot lift the earth that has barred him in. His situation is humanly impossible — which is exactly the point.
elements.questionHint: Try to trace the movement of the images — do they go deeper or shallower as the verses progress? What effect does this have?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The language Jonah uses (‘depths of the grave’, ‘the pit’, ‘barred me in for ever’) is the language of death. Why might it be important that his rescue comes from what is functionally a dead-end situation?

elements.body: When you are out of your depth, you need help. The greater the trouble, the greater the help you need.

elements.question: Jonah says in v4, ‘I have been banished from your sight; yet I will look again towards your holy temple.’ What shift does this single verse mark in the prayer?
elements.sampleAnswer: V4 is the hinge of the whole prayer. The first half — ‘I have been banished from your sight’ — expresses Jonah’s felt reality: he is cut off, far from God, apparently abandoned. But the second half — ‘yet I will look again towards your holy temple’ — marks a turn. Despite feeling banished, Jonah resolves to look toward God. He does not wait for his feelings to change before he prays; he prays against the grain of his feelings. The word ‘yet’ is everything: it holds together the honest acknowledgment of how things feel with a deliberate decision to trust anyway. This is not denial — Jonah does not pretend things are fine. It is faith: choosing to orient toward God even when circumstances say there is no point.
elements.questionHint: The word ‘yet’ carries a lot of weight here. What two things is it holding together, and why does that small word matter?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Have you ever prayed against the grain of your feelings — choosing to trust God when circumstances suggested you shouldn’t? What did that look like, and what came of it?

elements.text: Calling to God for Help (2:7)

elements.passageText: "7When my life was ebbing away, I remembered you, Lord, and my prayer rose to you, to your holy temple."

elements.question: V7 says, ‘When my life was ebbing away, I remembered you, Lord.’ Why did it take this to make Jonah call out to God? What does this tell us about how we typically respond to trouble?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah should have called to God long before this — he should never have run away in the first place. But even once the storm came, he slept through it rather than praying. It takes the absolute extremity — his life literally ebbing away, seconds from death — to make him remember God and call out. This is a damning indictment, but it is also painfully recognisable. Most of us are tempted to try everything else first: our own resources, other people’s help, ignoring the problem — and to turn to God only when all of that has failed. We treat prayer as a last resort rather than a first response. The wartime proverb applies: there are no atheists in foxholes. Desperation, as uncomfortable as it is, has a way of clarifying what we actually believe and who we actually trust.
elements.questionHint: Jonah had plenty of earlier opportunities to call out to God — before he got on the boat, during the storm, when the captain woke him. What kept him from it until now?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Do you tend to pray as a first response to trouble, or as a last resort? What does your pattern of prayer reveal about where you actually place your trust?

elements.body: Are you facing trouble at the moment? Have you called out to God for help?

elements.question: Jonah calls from the depths of the grave and God listens (v2). What does this tell us about the reach of God — and about what kind of situations it is worth praying in?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s situation is, by any human measure, completely hopeless. He is at the bottom of the sea, inside a fish, having been thrown overboard in a violent storm. There is nowhere lower to be, and no human mechanism of rescue. And yet God hears him. The Lord listens to a prayer prayed from the depths of the grave. This tells us something important about God’s reach: there is no situation so extreme, no place so remote, no depth so unreachable, that it puts the person there beyond God’s hearing. Nowhere is beyond God’s reach. If God can hear Jonah from there — from the inside of a fish at the bottom of the sea — then no situation we face is too far gone to pray about. It is never pointless to call out to God.
elements.questionHint: The phrase ‘depths of the grave’ is not just poetic — it describes the furthest possible point from life and from God’s presence. What does it mean that God hears from there?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Is there a situation in your life — or someone else’s — that feels too far gone to pray about? How does Jonah’s experience speak to that?

elements.body: The thing that makes the difference is: what are we focusing on? If you have a decent-sized problem and you focus on it, it will seem to get bigger and bigger. But if you focus on the Lord our God, the problem will seem small by comparison.

elements.text: Idols, Grace, and Salvation (2:8–9)

elements.passageText: "8Those who cling to worthless idols turn away from God’s love for them. 9But I, with shouts of grateful praise, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. I will say, ‘Salvation comes from the Lord.’"

elements.question: V8 says those who cling to worthless idols forfeit God’s grace. Given what happened in chapter 1, what is ironic about Jonah saying this?
elements.sampleAnswer: The irony is sharp. In chapter 1, the sailors began the story crying out to their various gods — the idols Jonah is now denouncing. But by the end of the chapter, they had turned away from those idols to worship the Lord. Jonah, meanwhile — the prophet who knew the true God — was the one who ran away from him, refusing to obey, effectively clinging to his own stubborn will rather than trusting God. In a sense, Jonah was the one forfeiting grace, not the pagan sailors. They were the ones who turned toward God; he was the one who turned away. So v8 is perfectly true — and perfectly, uncomfortably applicable to Jonah himself. He is preaching a sermon he has just been living out from the wrong side.
elements.questionHint: Think back to chapter 1: who was actually clinging to idols, and who was turning toward the Lord? Does Jonah realise the irony of what he is saying?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: An idol does not have to be a wooden statue. What are the ‘worthless idols’ — the things we cling to instead of God — that are most tempting in your own life? What grace might be forfeited by clinging to them?

elements.question: V9 ends with the declaration ‘Salvation comes from the Lord.’ Jonah knew this was true before any of this happened. What is the difference between knowing a truth and having lived it? What has changed for Jonah?
elements.sampleAnswer: ‘Salvation comes from the Lord’ is not a new theological discovery for Jonah. He knew this before he got on the boat for Tarshish. He knew it before the storm. He knew it before the fish. But knowing a truth in your head and having it formed in your life through experience are two very different things. Jonah knew salvation came from God — but he acted as though it came from his own choices and his own ability to manage his situation. He ran. He slept. He tried to exit the problem rather than turn back to God. What has changed is that he has now been on the receiving end of that salvation in the most visceral possible way. He was seconds from death; God pulled him out. He can no longer treat ‘salvation comes from the Lord’ as a slogan. It is now a fact he has tasted. The question the book leaves open is: will he live differently because of it?
elements.questionHint: Jonah is not learning a new theological truth here — he already knew this. So what is the difference between knowing it before and saying it now?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Is there a truth about God that you know in your head but have not yet fully lived? What would it look like for that truth to move from intellectual knowledge to something that actually shapes your behaviour?

elements.text: Thankfulness — and What Comes Next (2:9–10)

elements.passageText: "9But I, with shouts of grateful praise, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. I will say, ‘Salvation comes from the Lord.’" 10And the Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

elements.question: Jonah prays a prayer of thankfulness from inside a fish. What is remarkable about that — and what does it tell us about what genuine thankfulness looks like?
elements.sampleAnswer: Being swallowed by a fish cannot be enjoyable. It is dark, smelly, slimy, and lonely. There is nothing obviously comfortable or pleasant about Jonah’s circumstances. And yet he gives thanks. What is remarkable is that he sees the fish clearly: not as a punishment or a disaster, but as a gracious rescue. He deserved to drown. God saved him — in an undignified, uncomfortable, unlikely way — but saved him nonetheless. Jonah is clear-sighted enough to see the grace in it, and he gives thanks for it. This tells us something important about genuine thankfulness: it does not require comfortable circumstances. It requires a clear view of what God has actually done. The alternative — ‘well, I’m alive, I suppose, but is this really the best you can do, Lord?’ — is ingratitude born of taking grace for granted.
elements.questionHint: God’s ‘help’ here was a fish that swallowed Jonah. Most of us would not instinctively be grateful for that. What does it take to see grace in an uncomfortable rescue?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God’s top priority for us is not a trouble-free life but a godly one. How does that reframe what we should be thankful for — and what should we do when God’s help does not look the way we expected?

elements.body:
What was the last thing you thanked God for?

Are there other things you could thank him for?

elements.question: V10 is short: ‘And the Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.’ What does God’s response to Jonah’s prayer tell us about how God treats people who have failed him and then turned back?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s response is immediate and complete: he commands the fish, and Jonah is deposited on dry land. There is no long probationary period, no ‘we’ll see if you can be trusted’, no grudging half-measure. Jonah prays with thankfulness and vows, and God acts. This is a picture of God’s extraordinary generosity toward people who have failed him. Jonah was wilfully disobedient. He ran. He slept through a storm he caused. He said nothing to the sailors about his God until they dragged it out of him. He deserved to drown. And yet: one prayer of genuine thankfulness and trust, and God delivers him completely. This is not what Jonah deserves — it is grace. It is also a gentle warning about what lies ahead: God’s generosity toward Jonah should make Jonah generous toward Nineveh. The question is whether it will.
elements.questionHint: God does not make Jonah wait or prove himself before responding. What does the immediacy of God’s response tell us about the nature of his forgiveness?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God’s rescue here is in ‘two stages’: first the fish, then dry land. Why might God sometimes help in stages rather than all at once? What might he be teaching in the in-between times?

elements.body:
It is not just Jonah who makes the mistake of knowing the truth and not living it out. We can know what God has done for us in Jesus, and still not show it in our lives. Jesus himself warned:

‘Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man who built his house on the rock … But everyone who hears these words of mine and does not put them into practice is like a foolish man who built his house on sand.’ (Matthew 7:24–27)

The question is worth sitting with: Am I living up to the knowledge I have? Am I hearing Jesus’ words and building on them — or am I just hearing?

elements.text: Reflection

elements.passageText: 1From inside the fish Jonah prayed to the Lord his God. 2He said: "In my distress I called to the Lord, and he answered me. From the depths of the grave I called for help, and you listened to my cry."

elements.question: Jonah called to God only when there was literally nowhere else to turn. Are there situations in your life right now where you have been trying everything except prayer? What would it look like to call out to God first, rather than last?
elements.sampleAnswer: Most of us have a natural order of operations when things go wrong: we try to sort it ourselves first, then ask others for help, and only turn to God when all else has failed. Jonah’s pattern is an extreme version of this — he literally had to be at the point of drowning inside a fish before he remembered to pray. The question is personal and practical: what is the current situation where the Ostrich Trick has been running? What would it look like to stop, bring it honestly before God, and ask for his help? Prayer as a first response rather than a last resort is not just piety — it is a statement about where we believe help actually comes from. If salvation truly comes from the Lord, then it makes sense to go to him first.
elements.questionHint: Try to name something specific — a relationship, a decision, a worry — that you have been managing on your own. What would bringing it to God look like this week?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: What stops us from praying first? Is it that we don’t really believe God can help, or that we want to stay in control, or something else? What does your answer reveal about where you actually place your trust?

elements.passageText: "…I said, ‘I have been banished from your sight; yet I will look again towards your holy temple.’"

elements.question: Jonah prays ‘yet I will look again towards your holy temple’ — choosing to orient toward God against the grain of how things felt. Is there something you need to pray about even though it feels pointless or too late? What would it mean to say ‘yet’ in your current situation?
elements.sampleAnswer: The word ‘yet’ is one of the most important words in the whole of Jonah’s prayer. It does not deny the reality of how things feel — Jonah genuinely feels banished, cut off, beyond hope. But it refuses to let that feeling be the final word. Faith, as this verse shows it, is not the absence of doubt or fear. It is the decision to orient toward God anyway — even when feelings say there is no point, even when circumstances say it is too late. The question is personal: what is the thing in your life right now where the feeling is ‘too far gone’, ‘too late’, or ‘God probably isn’t interested in this’? Jonah’s ‘yet’ is an invitation to pray that prayer anyway.
elements.questionHint: Jonah prays this while still inside the fish — before anything has changed in his circumstances. What does that tell us about the relationship between prayer and our feelings?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Where does the confidence to say ‘yet’ come from? What has to be true about God for that word to be possible in the middle of a hopeless situation?

elements.passageText: "9But I, with shouts of grateful praise, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. I will say, ‘Salvation comes from the Lord.’"

elements.question: ‘Salvation comes from the Lord’ — Jonah knew this before he ran, but had not lived it. Is there a truth about God that you know well but have not yet let shape the way you actually live? What would it look like to start building on it this week?
elements.sampleAnswer: The gap between knowing a truth and living it is one of the most common and honest struggles in the Christian life. Jonah is not unusual in this — he is representative. He knew exactly who God was and exactly what God required. That knowledge did not automatically translate into obedience. The Making Connections section draws the same line to Jesus’ words: hearing is not the same as building. The question is specific and practical: not ‘is there a gap?’ (there always is), but ‘where is the gap right now, and what is one concrete step toward closing it?’ It might be a truth about forgiveness that has not yet reached a relationship. A truth about contentment that has not yet reached a habit. A truth about trusting God that has not yet reached a decision you are avoiding. Jonah’s experience inside the fish is a vivid reminder that the gap tends to get painfully obvious eventually — it is better to close it now.
elements.questionHint: Try to be specific: name the truth, and name the gap between knowing it and living it. Then name one small, concrete step toward closing that gap this week.

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Why is it not enough simply to know the right things about God? What does it take for head-knowledge to become lived reality — and what role does experience (including uncomfortable experience) play in that process?

elements.text: Have you ever felt like you wanted a second chance at something? Second chances can be wonderful — especially when the problem was created by your own moral failures. Life does not always give you a second chance. But sometimes it does, and when it does, it is worth grabbing hold of it with both hands.

elements.body: What do you wish you could have a second chance at?

elements.question: Read the whole of chapter 3 before you begin…
elements.answer:
1Then the word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and proclaim to it the message I give you."

3Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh. Now Nineveh was a very important city — a visit required three days. 4On the first day, Jonah started into the city. He proclaimed: "Forty more days and Nineveh will be destroyed."

5The Ninevites believed God. They declared a fast, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth.

6When the news reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his royal robes, covered himself with sackcloth and sat down in the dust. 7Then he issued a proclamation in Nineveh:

"By the decree of the king and his nobles: Do not let any man or beast, herd or flock, taste anything; do not let them eat or drink. 8But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth. Let everyone call urgently on God. Let them give up their evil ways and their violence. 9Who knows? God may yet relent and with compassion turn from his fierce anger so that we will not perish."

10When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened.

elements.text: As you read, notice how quickly things move in this chapter — and pay attention to the contrast between who you might expect to respond to God and who actually does.

elements.question: What surprises you most about this chapter? What did you expect, and what actually happened?

elements.text: Scene 1 — Jonah’s Second Chance (3:1–3)

elements.passageText: 1Then the word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and proclaim to it the message I give you." 3Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh. Now Nineveh was a very important city — a visit required three days.

elements.question: Compare v1–2 with 1:1–2. What is almost identical? What does the repetition tell us about God?
elements.sampleAnswer: The two openings are almost word for word identical: the word of the Lord comes to Jonah, and the command is the same — go to Nineveh, proclaim the message. God has not changed the mission. He has not found a more compliant prophet. He has not abandoned Jonah. He simply comes back with the same call. This tells us something important about God: he does not give up on people who have failed him. He could have said, ‘You had your chance — you’re finished as a prophet.’ That would have been understandable, perhaps even deserved. Instead he comes back with grace and a renewed commission. The God of the second chance is not a God who lowers his standards; he is a God who keeps coming back to people who have walked away.
elements.questionHint: Read 1:1–2 alongside 3:1–2. What has changed and what has stayed exactly the same? What does the unchanged commission tell us?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God gives Jonah a second chance despite his spectacular failure. What does this tell us about how God deals with people who disobey him — and what does it imply about how we should deal with people who fail us?

elements.body: God is not a ‘one-chance’ God. He is not even merely a ‘second-chance’ God. God is hugely generous and gives us many, many chances. That is what ‘God is gracious’ means.

elements.question: V3 simply says ‘Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh.’ Given what we know about Jonah from chapters 1 and 2, what might this have cost him? What do you think changed?
elements.sampleAnswer: The text is spare — it does not tell us about Jonah’s inner state. But we know from chapter 4 that his original fears and objections have not gone away; he still does not want the Ninevites to repent, still resents God’s potential compassion toward them. So his obedience is not a change of heart — it is a change of behaviour. What changed is that his experience in the fish — three days of reflection in the most uncomfortable circumstances imaginable — has recalibrated his sense of what happens when you run from God. He has learned that the call does not go away, that God is more tenacious than he is, and that the cost of disobedience is very high. He obeys. Perhaps reluctantly, perhaps still with all his feelings intact. But he goes. That is itself worth noting: faithful obedience does not always require sorted feelings first.
elements.questionHint: V3 describes what Jonah did, not how he felt about it. What does the gap between his behaviour and his inner state (visible in chapter 4) tell us about the nature of obedience?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Is there value in obedience that is not accompanied by the right feelings — or does that make it hollow? What do you think Jonah’s case suggests?

elements.text: Scene 2 — The Shortest Sermon (3:4)

elements.passageText: 4On the first day, Jonah started into the city. He proclaimed: "Forty more days and Nineveh will be destroyed."

elements.question: Jonah’s message is five words in the original Hebrew: ‘Forty more days and Nineveh will be destroyed.’ It contains no explicit offer of mercy, no call to repent. What is the message actually communicating — and what is implicit in the very fact that it was sent at all?
elements.sampleAnswer: On the surface the message is pure judgment: a countdown to destruction, nothing more. But the king of Nineveh, remarkably, sees what is implicit in it. If God simply wanted to destroy Nineveh, there was no need to send a warning. The very fact that God sent a prophet with a message — even a message of judgment — is itself an act of grace. A warning implies that there is still time, still the possibility of a different outcome. The king’s response (‘Who knows? God may yet relent’) shows he understood this. God’s ‘Final Warning’ in big red letters is also, implicitly, an invitation. Jonah’s minimal, reluctant sermon communicates more than he perhaps intended.
elements.questionHint: If God simply wanted to destroy Nineveh, why send a prophet? What does the act of warning — even without an explicit offer of mercy — imply about God’s intentions?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: How would you feel if you knew you would have to answer to God in forty days’ time? What would you do differently? What does your answer reveal about what you actually believe about God?

elements.body:
How would you feel if you knew you would have to answer to God in 40 days’ time?

What would you do?

elements.text: Scene 3 — Nineveh Takes Its Second Chance (3:5–9)

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>The Ninevites believed God. They declared a fast, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth.

elements.question: V5 says ‘The Ninevites believed God.’ What does it mean to ‘believe God’ — and what three things does this phrase imply they believed?
elements.sampleAnswer: To ‘believe God’ means to take God at his word — to treat what he says as true and act accordingly. In this context it implies at least three things. First, they believe in the God Jonah proclaims — they accept that this God is real and has authority over their city. Second, they believe God is right to pronounce judgment — they recognise that they deserve condemnation, that their wickedness has genuinely offended him. Third, they believe God means what he says — the threat is real, the forty days are real, the destruction is real unless something changes. All three are forms of taking God seriously. The Ninevites, who had never heard of this God before Jonah arrived, manage in a single day to do what Jonah — who had known God his whole life — had failed to do: they take God at his word and act on it.
elements.questionHint: The phrase ‘believed God’ is different from ‘believed in God.’ What does the specific phrasing suggest about the nature of their response?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The Ninevites believed God on the basis of five words from a reluctant prophet. What does that tell us about how little God needs to work with in order to bring people to faith?

elements.body: Sin means rebellion. God made this world, he made the Ninevites, and he made you and me. God has things to say about how we ought to live in his world. Sin is all about us saying, ‘Well God, it’s nice that you think we should do such-and-such, but actually we think we will do this other thing instead.’ Sin is about living in God’s world without paying him any real attention at all.

elements.passageText: 6When the news reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his royal robes, covered himself with sackcloth and sat down in the dust. 7Then he issued a proclamation in Nineveh: "By the decree of the king and his nobles: Do not let any man or beast, herd or flock, taste anything; do not let them eat or drink. 8But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth. Let everyone call urgently on God. Let them give up their evil ways and their violence. 9Who knows? God may yet relent and with compassion turn from his fierce anger so that we will not perish."

elements.question: The king’s decree describes a three-stage process: recognising sin, expressing sorrow, and changing behaviour. Why do all three matter? What would be missing if any one of them were absent?
elements.sampleAnswer: The three stages — sin, sorrow, sackcloth — are each necessary, and the absence of any one would reveal something was missing. If there is no recognition of sin, there is no reason to seek forgiveness — a person who does not think they are sinful does not think they need to be forgiven. If there is recognition but no sorrow, the response is merely intellectual — ‘Yes, I suppose I have done wrong’ said with a shrug. Plenty of people are willing to admit they are sinful but have no real sense that it matters. If there is sorrow but no change of behaviour, the sorrow is just emotion without weight — feeling bad without doing anything about it. The king’s decree shows all three working together: they know they have sinned, they are genuinely cut to the heart by it, and their sorrow translates into the concrete action of fasting, sackcloth, and calling urgently on God. The genuineness of their response is visible precisely because it changes what they do.
elements.questionHint: Think of someone who admits to wrongdoing but shows no sorrow about it — and someone who is sorry but never changes. What is missing in each case?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The king says ‘Who knows? God may yet relent.’ He is not certain of the outcome but responds anyway. What does it say about genuine repentance that it does not wait for guaranteed results before acting?

elements.body: The Ninevites went through three stages — three S-words: 1. Recognise sin. If we do not think we are sinful, we will not think we need to be forgiven. 2. Be sorry about it. Realising sin is a bad thing — not just admitting it, but caring that it is wrong. 3. Respond to God. The one action God wants from us is this: trust in his Son. Lots more actions will flow from that. But trusting Jesus Christ is the make-or-break issue.

Have you recognised your own sin?

Do you care that it is wrong?

Have you turned to God for forgiveness?

elements.text: Scene 4 — God Has Compassion on Nineveh (3:10)

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened.

elements.question: V10 says God ‘had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened.’ What does this tell us about what kind of God he is? Does this mean God changes his mind?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s response to Nineveh’s repentance reveals something fundamental about his character: he is not a mechanical God who executes judgments regardless of how people respond. He cares deeply about how people respond to him. He would far rather see people turn from evil and live than carry out the destruction they deserve. Jeremiah 18:7–8 makes this explicit: God’s announced judgments are conditional on the response of the people. This is not God being fickle or unpredictable. It is God being consistent in his character — he is a God who loves to show compassion. What changes when Nineveh repents is not God’s character but the situation: the people who were living in rebellious wickedness are now turning from it. God responds to that change. He is no computer executing a program; he is a person who takes seriously how we respond to him. The Ninevites had no guarantee of mercy — the king said ‘who knows?’ — and yet God’s response to genuine repentance is exactly what you would hope.
elements.questionHint: The king of Nineveh says ‘who knows? God may yet relent.’ He is not certain. What does God’s actual response tell us about what he is like — and how reliable that ‘may’ really is?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Read Jeremiah 18:7–8. How does this passage help us understand God’s response in Jonah 3:10? What does it tell us about the relationship between God’s announced judgments and human repentance?

elements.body: God is not some computer that responds blindly and automatically. He cares about how we respond to him and takes it very seriously. No matter how bad someone is, God does not want to see them destroyed. He would far rather see them turn to him and live.

elements.question: The Ninevites were brutal war criminals — among the most notorious in the ancient world. God had compassion on them when they repented. What does this tell us about the limits (or lack of limits) of God’s willingness to forgive?
elements.sampleAnswer: If God can have compassion on the Ninevites — people whose wickedness was of a particularly horrific, systematic, and deliberate kind — then there is no category of person or sin that is simply beyond his willingness to forgive. This is both wonderful and, as the goDeeperData notes, ‘amazingly costly news.’ Forgiveness is not free for God. He does not look the other way or pretend sin does not matter. God cannot turn a blind eye to evil and still be God — evil has to be paid for. What the Ninevites did not know, and what Jonah himself did not yet understand, is that God was going to pay that price himself. Jesus took the sin of people like the Ninevites — and like us — on himself. The reason God can extend mercy to those who repent is that justice has not been abandoned; it has been satisfied at enormous cost.
elements.questionHint: Is there anyone, in your mind, who would be too bad for God to forgive? How does Nineveh’s story challenge that instinct?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Read Romans 3:25–26. How does Paul explain how God can be both just and the one who forgives guilty people? What does the cross have to do with the forgiveness of Nineveh?

elements.body:
God presented Jesus as a sacrifice of atonement … He did this to demonstrate his justice … so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus. (Romans 3:25–26)

Forgiveness is not easy or cheap for God. It is supremely costly — it cost the life of his one and only Son. The reason God can give us so many chances to turn from sin is that Jesus has bought those chances with his blood. That is wonderfully good news, but it is also wonderfully serious news. The Ninevites had only a one-sentence message to go on, and they took it seriously. Let’s be sure we take the message of Jesus seriously too.

elements.text: Reflection

elements.passageText: 1Then the word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and proclaim to it the message I give you." 3Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh. Now Nineveh was a very important city — a visit required three days.

elements.question: God gave Jonah a second chance and came back with the same call. Is there something you know God has been asking of you — something you have been running from or putting off — that he keeps coming back to? What would it look like to respond this time?
elements.sampleAnswer: The pattern in chapter 3 is striking: the call comes again, unchanged. God does not negotiate a smaller version of the mission or settle for a partial response. He comes back with the same thing he asked for in the first place. Most of us have experienced something similar: a persistent sense that God is pointing at something — a relationship that needs repairing, a conversation that needs to be had, a step of obedience that keeps presenting itself. And like Jonah, we often have very good reasons for why this time is not the right time. The question this passage asks is whether we will take the second chance — and the third, and the fourth — or whether, like Jonah, we will need to be swallowed by a fish first. The call will not change. The question is whether we will respond to it.
elements.questionHint: The commission in 3:1–2 is almost word-for-word the same as 1:1–2. God did not change the ask. What does that tell you about the thing you have been avoiding?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Jonah obeyed without his feelings catching up to his behaviour. Is there something you need to do in obedience to God even though your feelings are not quite there yet? What would taking the first step look like?

elements.passageText: 5The Ninevites believed God. They declared a fast, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth. 6When the news reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his royal robes, covered himself with sackcloth and sat down in the dust. 7Then he issued a proclamation in Nineveh: "By the decree of the king and his nobles: Do not let any man or beast, herd or flock, taste anything; do not let them eat or drink. 8But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth. Let everyone call urgently on God. Let them give up their evil ways and their violence. 9Who knows? God may yet relent and with compassion turn from his fierce anger so that we will not perish."

elements.question: The Ninevites went through three stages: recognising their sin, being genuinely sorry about it, and responding to God. Have you been through that process? Is there a stage you have stopped at — or never fully reached?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is one of the most searching questions in the whole study. The three stages are simple to describe and surprisingly easy to stop short of. Some people recognise they are sinful in a general, abstract sense but have never brought specific sins honestly before God. Some feel genuine sorrow — perhaps over the consequences of their sin more than the sin itself — but have never actually turned to God and asked for forgiveness. Some have done all three at some point in the past but have become comfortable since, and the process has stalled. The Ninevites’ response was immediate, urgent, and total — ‘from the greatest to the least.’ Chapter 3 is an invitation to ask honestly: where am I in that process? And is there a next step?
elements.questionHint: Be honest about which stage feels most real to you and which feels most distant. Is it recognising sin, caring about it, or actually turning to God?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The king of Nineveh acted without a guarantee (‘who knows?’). What would it mean for you to turn to God without waiting for certainty that it will ‘work out’ first?

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened.

elements.question: God had compassion on people who fully deserved destruction. Is there someone in your life — or a group of people — whom you have written off as too far gone for God? How does Nineveh’s story challenge that?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah himself is the most obvious cautionary example: he knew God was compassionate and it made him angry. He had mentally written off the Ninevites. They did not deserve grace, and he was not prepared to be the instrument of it. Most of us have, somewhere in our thinking, a category of person or group that we have quietly concluded is beyond God’s reach — too hardened, too hostile, too far into a pattern of life that seems irreversible. Nineveh’s story is a direct challenge to that category. If God can bring 120,000 Assyrian war criminals to repentance through a single reluctant sentence from an unwilling prophet, he can reach anyone. The question Jonah’s story raises is not whether God is willing — he clearly is — but whether we are willing to be instruments of that reach, even toward people we would rather see judged.
elements.questionHint: Is there a person, a people group, or a type of person you have privately concluded is beyond God’s compassion? What would it look like to pray for them in the way that God’s compassion in chapter 3 invites you to?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Jonah preached to the Ninevites while privately hoping they would not repent. Is there anyone you find it hard to genuinely want God’s mercy for? What would it take to pray for their repentance and mean it?

elements.text: Chapter 4 is deliberately unsettling. If we had stopped at the end of chapter 3, we would have had a perfectly tidy, happy ending: the reluctant prophet preaches, a whole city repents, God shows compassion. But the book does not stop there. The final chapter rips that tidy ending apart — and in doing so, it forces us to ask some uncomfortable questions about ourselves.

elements.question: Read the whole of chapter 4 before you begin…
elements.answer:
1But Jonah was greatly displeased and became angry. 2He prayed to the Lord, "O Lord, is this not what I said when I was still at home? That is why I was so quick to flee to Tarshish. I knew that you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love, a God who relents from sending calamity. 3Now, O Lord, take away my life, for it is better for me to die than to live."

4But the Lord replied, "Have you any right to be angry?"

5Jonah went out and sat down at a place east of the city. There he made himself a shelter, sat in its shade and waited to see what would happen to the city. 6Then the Lord God provided a vine and made it grow up over Jonah to give shade for his head to ease his discomfort, and Jonah was very happy about the vine. 7But at dawn the next day God provided a worm, which chewed the vine so that it withered. 8When the sun rose, God provided a scorching east wind, and the sun blazed on Jonah’s head so that he grew faint. He wanted to die, and said, "It would be better for me to die than to live."

9But God said to Jonah, "Do you have a right to be angry about the vine?" "I do," he said. "I am angry enough to die."

10But the Lord said, "You have been concerned about this vine, though you did not tend it or make it grow. It sprang up overnight and died overnight. 11But Nineveh has more than a hundred and twenty thousand people who cannot tell their right hand from their left, and many cattle as well. Should I not be concerned about that great city?"

elements.text: As you read, pay attention to the two questions God asks Jonah — and notice what Jonah says, and what he doesn’t say.

elements.question: How did you react to Jonah’s response in vv1–3? Did it surprise you — or, on reflection, does it make a kind of sense?

elements.text: Scene 1 — Aiming to Fail (3:10–4:4)

elements.passageText: <sup>3:10</sup>When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened. <sup>4:1</sup>But Jonah was greatly displeased and became angry.

elements.question: Read 3:10–4:1 together as one sentence. Jonah’s mission has just been an extraordinary success. Why is he angry? What does this reveal about what he actually wanted?
elements.sampleAnswer: The juxtaposition is jarring. God had compassion; Jonah became angry. The very outcome that should have delighted a prophet of God — 120,000 people turning from wickedness, destruction averted, God’s mercy on display — is the thing that enrages Jonah. This reveals something that the book has been carefully concealing until now: Jonah did not want his mission to succeed. He went to Nineveh, but he went hoping for failure. He delivered the message, but he wanted them to ignore it. He was not a prophet who reluctantly preached despite his fears; he was a prophet who preached while secretly praying the opposite of what his message implied. His anger at the outcome shows us what was in his heart all along: he wanted the Ninevites destroyed, not saved.
elements.questionHint: What would Jonah have considered a ‘successful’ outcome for his mission? How does the answer reveal what he was really after?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Have you ever found yourself hoping that a person or a group of people would fail, be punished, or not receive God’s mercy? How does Jonah’s story speak to that instinct?

elements.passageText: 1But Jonah was greatly displeased and became angry. 2He prayed to the Lord, "O Lord, is this not what I said when I was still at home? That is why I was so quick to flee to Tarshish. I knew that you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love, a God who relents from sending calamity. 3Now, O Lord, take away my life, for it is better for me to die than to live." 4But the Lord replied, "Have you any right to be angry?"

elements.question: In v2, Jonah finally tells us why he ran in chapter 1. What was his real reason? What does it tell us about Jonah’s view of God — and of himself?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s real reason is finally out: he was not afraid of the Ninevites. He was afraid that God would be merciful to them if they repented. He ran because he knew God’s character — gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in love, relenting from calamity — and he did not want that character exercised toward Nineveh. The irony is that the very qualities of God that Jonah quotes here are among the most beloved in the Old Testament. They echo the great self-revelation of God in Exodus 34:6–7. Jonah knows them, has presumably been comforted by them in his own life — and now he throws them back at God as a complaint. This tells us that Jonah has one set of rules for himself and another for his enemies. He is happy for God to be gracious to Israel, but considers God’s grace toward Nineveh an outrage. His view of himself is that he deserves God’s mercy; his view of the Ninevites is that they do not.
elements.questionHint: The qualities Jonah lists in v2 — gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in love — are usually things we praise God for. Why is Jonah using them as a complaint?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Look up Exodus 34:6–7 — the original context where God reveals these qualities. What is Jonah doing by quoting this passage in complaint? What does it tell us about the difference between knowing God’s character and accepting its implications?

elements.body: Jonah cannot stand the Ninevites. But it is worse than that: to a shocking extent, Jonah cannot even stand God. He complains that God is just the wrong sort of God — gracious and compassionate, of all things. Where is the sense in a God who keeps holding back his condemnation from horrible people who deserve it?

elements.question: Jonah says he would rather die than see God show mercy to Nineveh (v3). God responds with a question: ‘Have you any right to be angry?’ (v4). Why is this question so devastating? And why does Jonah say nothing in reply?
elements.sampleAnswer: The question is devastating because of everything that has happened to Jonah in chapters 1 and 2. He disobeyed God, caused a storm that nearly killed innocent sailors, was thrown overboard, and should have drowned. God saved him with a fish. He has received mercy he did not deserve, rescue he had not earned, and a second chance he had no right to expect. For Jonah to now be furious that God is showing the same kind of mercy to Nineveh is breathtaking in its hypocrisy. He has experienced God’s free grace in his own life and is outraged when God extends it to others. He has no answer because there is no answer. He cannot claim a right to be angry without implicitly claiming a right to God’s mercy that he knows he does not have. His silence is the silence of someone who has been caught in their own logic.
elements.questionHint: Think about what God has done for Jonah in chapters 1–2. How does that history make God’s question in v4 impossible to answer?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God’s question — ‘Have you any right to be angry?’ — could be asked of us in many situations. Are there circumstances in your life where you are angry at God’s mercy toward someone else? How does Jonah’s silence speak to that?

elements.body: Our world often tells us that feelings are always trustworthy: ‘If you feel it, then it’s true for you.’ But as Christians we know our thoughts can be right or wrong, sinful or godly — and so can our words and deeds. The same is true of feelings. Jonah’s feelings here are wrong, and he needs to repent of them. Any time we find our feelings are the exact opposite of how God has said he feels about something, it should give us enormous pause for thought.

elements.text: Scene 2 — The Vine, the Worm, and the Wind (4:5–11)

elements.passageText: 5Jonah went out and sat down at a place east of the city. There he made himself a shelter, sat in its shade and waited to see what would happen to the city. 6Then the Lord God provided a vine and made it grow up over Jonah to give shade for his head to ease his discomfort, and Jonah was very happy about the vine. 7But at dawn the next day God provided a worm, which chewed the vine so that it withered. 8When the sun rose, God provided a scorching east wind, and the sun blazed on Jonah’s head so that he grew faint. He wanted to die, and said, "It would be better for me to die than to live."

elements.question: V6 says Jonah was ‘very happy’ about the vine. This is the only time in the whole book Jonah is described as happy. What does that tell us about the state of his heart throughout?
elements.sampleAnswer: It is a striking and rather damning detail. The only thing that makes Jonah happy in the entire story is a plant that provides him personal comfort. Not the rescue from drowning in chapter 2. Not the second chance in chapter 3. Not the extraordinary sight of 120,000 people turning to God. A vine. This tells us that throughout the story Jonah has been largely self-focused. His misery in chapter 1 was about being asked to do something he did not want to do. His prayer in chapter 2, for all its genuine theology, was focused on his own rescue. His anger in chapter 4 is about his enemies getting what he thinks they do not deserve. And his happiness is about his own physical comfort. The vine making him ‘very happy’ sets up the object lesson perfectly: God is about to expose exactly how self-centred Jonah’s concerns have been.
elements.questionHint: Go back through the whole story: when else does Jonah express happiness or contentment? What does the absence of joy at Nineveh’s repentance tell us?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Jonah was happy about something that served him personally and indifferent to something that served others eternally. How does that contrast sit with you? Where do you notice similar priorities in your own life?

elements.question: God provided the vine, and then God provided the worm. What is God doing with this sequence — and why does it lead straight into his question in v9?
elements.sampleAnswer: God is setting up an object lesson, almost like a parable made out of real events. He gives Jonah the vine so that Jonah becomes genuinely attached to it — it matters to him, it brings him comfort and joy. Then he takes it away, to expose how Jonah responds to loss and frustration. The sequence works because Jonah’s reaction to the vine’s death is so disproportionate: he wants to die over a plant. That reaction then becomes the measuring stick. If Jonah can be ‘angry enough to die’ over a vine he had no part in creating or sustaining, then God’s question about Nineveh carries enormous force: is it possible that 120,000 people might deserve at least as much concern as the plant? The sequence is designed to let Jonah condemn himself with his own words and his own feelings.
elements.questionHint: Notice that God ‘provided’ both the vine and the worm. He is still in control. What does the sequence reveal about what God is trying to show Jonah?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God uses an ordinary object — a plant — to make a profound point. Can you think of a time when God used something small and ordinary to show you something important about yourself?

elements.passageText: 9But God said to Jonah, "Do you have a right to be angry about the vine?" "I do," he said. "I am angry enough to die." 10But the Lord said, "You have been concerned about this vine, though you did not tend it or make it grow. It sprang up overnight and died overnight. 11But Nineveh has more than a hundred and twenty thousand people who cannot tell their right hand from their left, and many cattle as well. Should I not be concerned about that great city?"
