elements.question: God’s final question — ‘Should I not be concerned about that great city?’ — is the last line of the book. There is no answer from Jonah, no tidy resolution. Why does the book end this way? What is it asking of the reader?
elements.sampleAnswer: The book ends with a question, not an answer, and that is almost certainly deliberate. We never find out whether Jonah repented, whether he changed, whether he eventually agreed with God. The story is left open. The effect is to turn the question on the reader: God has just asked Jonah whether he should be concerned about a city of 120,000 people. The obvious answer is yes. But the real question is whether the reader agrees — and whether they will respond differently from Jonah. The open ending also leaves Jonah’s story as a mirror rather than a cautionary tale with a neat moral: we are not invited to look at Jonah and congratulate ourselves that we are not like him. We are invited to sit with the discomfort of how much we are.
elements.questionHint: Why might the author have chosen not to tell us what Jonah said in response? What does the open ending do that a tidy conclusion would not?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: God’s question at the end of the book is ultimately about whether we share his concern for people who do not know him. How would you honestly answer that question about your own city, your own neighbourhood, your own workplace?

elements.body: Jonah insists in the strongest terms that the vine matters to him — it was a comfort and a delight, and its loss makes him angry enough to want to be dead. All this over a plant. Not a person, not a family, not a community, far less a whole city. And yet the Lord is only doing for Nineveh what Jonah has just insisted ought to be done for a plant: showing concern for it. How concerned are we for the cities around us? Do we see them as cities that God has concern for? Do we show as much concern for them as we do for our houseplants or pets?

elements.text: Learning to Be Passionate for Our World

elements.question: The goDeeperData suggests three ways to grow in compassion for those who don’t know God: remembering his grace to us, prayer, and supporting gospel proclamation. Why does each one flow naturally from the lessons of Jonah’s story?
elements.sampleAnswer: Remembering God’s grace to us flows directly from God’s question in v4: ‘Have you any right to be angry?’ Jonah’s problem was that he had received mercy and not let it change him. When you genuinely understand how much you have been forgiven, the natural response is to want others to experience the same thing. You do not hoard what you know is a gift. Prayer flows from the recognition that God cares about Nineveh — and our city — far more than Jonah did. If God is concerned about 120,000 people who don’t know their right hand from their left, then praying for people we know who don’t yet know God is simply aligning ourselves with what God already wants. Supporting gospel proclamation flows from Jonah’s own mission: God sent a messenger, and the message changed everything. The Ninevites had only a five-word sentence, and they responded. People in our world need someone to tell them too. Whether we go ourselves, support those who go, or create opportunities for others to hear — it all flows from taking seriously the concern God has for the people around us.
elements.questionHint: Think about each of the three: which one comes most naturally to you, and which one do you tend to neglect? What would it look like to grow in the one you neglect most?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Jonah knew about God’s grace but it had not really sunk in. Is there a difference in your own life between knowing you have been forgiven and living as if you have been? What would it look like for that grace to actually shape your attitude toward others?

elements.body:
We live on the other side of the coming of Jesus. That means we can know what it is to be forgiven through his death — and we have all the more reason to forgive others.

Forgiveness is not the same as reconciliation. Reconciliation is a two-way process — if the other person remains hostile, there is a limit to what you can do. But forgiveness does not require the other person. It is about us letting go of our grievances. God will sort out the rights and wrongs. Jesus calls us to forgive our enemies and pray for their welfare (Matthew 5:43–48).

What is your ‘long-term future’, beyond this life?

How does that shape the way you live now?

elements.text: Reflection

elements.passageText: "O Lord, is this not what I said when I was still at home? That is why I was so quick to flee to Tarshish. I knew that you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger and abounding in love, a God who relents from sending calamity." (4:2)

elements.question: Jonah received enormous mercy from God — and it did not change how he felt about his enemies receiving the same. Is there someone you struggle to want God’s mercy for? Someone whose repentance would secretly disappoint you? What does Jonah’s story say to that?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is perhaps the most uncomfortably personal question in the whole study. Jonah’s failure is not simply that he disobeyed; it is that he disobeyed because he could not bear the thought of his enemies being forgiven. That instinct — wanting justice for others and mercy for ourselves — is not unique to Jonah. Most of us have, somewhere, a person or a group whose repentance would feel unfair rather than joyful. Maybe someone who has wronged us personally. Maybe a category of people we have written off as beyond deserving grace. Jonah’s story exposes this as exactly the wrong response to having received mercy ourselves. The person who has been forgiven much — and who knows it — is the person most motivated to want others to experience that same forgiveness. The question is whether we have really let the depth of our own forgiveness sink in.
elements.questionHint: Be specific if you can. Is there a name, a face, a category of person? Bringing it into the open — at least before God — is the first step.

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: What would it take for you to genuinely pray for that person’s repentance and mean it? What would have to change in you — not in them?

elements.passageText: 9But God said to Jonah, "Do you have a right to be angry about the vine?" "I do," he said. "I am angry enough to die." 10But the Lord said, "You have been concerned about this vine, though you did not tend it or make it grow. It sprang up overnight and died overnight. 11But Nineveh has more than a hundred and twenty thousand people who cannot tell their right hand from their left, and many cattle as well. Should I not be concerned about that great city?"

elements.question: God’s final question is: ‘Should I not be concerned about that great city?’ Think about the city, town, or neighbourhood where you live. How much concern do you actually have for the people there who don’t know God? What is one concrete step toward sharing God’s concern?
elements.sampleAnswer: The question at the end of Jonah is deliberately left unanswered — because the answer is meant to come from us. God is concerned about Nineveh. Is he concerned about our city? Of course he is. The question is whether we are. Concern for people who don’t know God can feel abstract until it becomes specific. A specific person you know who doesn’t follow Jesus. A specific street or community you drive past. A specific prayer you could pray, by name, regularly. The study’s three suggestions — remembering grace, praying, supporting proclamation — all require some degree of specificity to move from good intention to action. The question is not whether you feel concerned enough — Jonah’s feelings never caught up with what God wanted. The question is whether you will act.
elements.questionHint: Try to name something specific: a person, a place, an action. Vague concern tends to stay vague. What would it look like to move it one step toward something concrete this week?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The book ends with a question and no answer from Jonah. If you were Jonah, what would you say? And what does your answer reveal about where you actually stand?

elements.passageText: "Now, O Lord, take away my life, for it is better for me to die than to live." But the Lord replied, "Have you any right to be angry?" (4:3–4)

elements.question: God asks Jonah ‘Have you any right to be angry?’ — and Jonah has no answer, because of what God has done for him. Is there an area of your life where your feelings toward God or toward someone else are out of joint with how God says he feels? What would it look like to bring those feelings to God honestly, and ask him to change them?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s feelings were wrong, and he needed to repent of them. The same can be true of us. Our feelings are not automatically trustworthy — they can reflect sin, pride, hurt, or self-centredness just as much as they can reflect genuine righteousness. The test the study suggests is simple: if my feeling is the exact opposite of how God says he feels about something, one of us has feelings that are out of joint. It is not God. Bringing wrong feelings to God honestly is itself an act of trust: ‘Lord, I know I feel angry/resentful/indifferent, and I know I shouldn’t. I can’t fix this feeling on my own. Change me.’ That is a prayer God can work with. Jonah’s mistake was not that he had wrong feelings; it was that he defended them rather than bringing them to God.
elements.questionHint: The study notes that feelings can be sinful just as much as thoughts, words, or deeds. Are there feelings you have been excusing rather than examining? What would it look like to bring them to God rather than defend them?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Looking back over all four chapters of Jonah: what is the single thing God most wants you to take away from this study? And what is one thing you will do differently because of it?

elements.text: We have seen the shock ending of the book — and that gives us the key to understanding Jonah’s odd behaviour throughout. He wants Nineveh to be destroyed. He wants God to pass judgment on the Ninevites now. He does not want them to have even a chance of repenting. With that vital information in place, we start to rewind the tape. We revisit earlier scenes and see if we can get a better grasp of what was really going on. We begin at the start of chapter 3 — immediately after the fish incident.

elements.body: Sessions 5–7 retrace our steps through Jonah — backwards. Each session revisits a chapter we have already studied, now with the knowledge of where the story ends. Session 5: Jonah 3. Session 6: Jonah 2. Session 7: Jonah 1. The ending changes everything.

elements.question: Re-read Jonah 3 before you begin — this time knowing that Jonah preached every word hoping it would fail…
elements.answer:
1Then the word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and proclaim to it the message I give you."

3Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh. Now Nineveh was a very important city — a visit required three days. 4On the first day, Jonah started into the city. He proclaimed: "Forty more days and Nineveh will be destroyed."

5The Ninevites believed God. They declared a fast, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth.

6When the news reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his royal robes, covered himself with sackcloth and sat down in the dust. 7Then he issued a proclamation in Nineveh:

"By the decree of the king and his nobles: Do not let any man or beast, herd or flock, taste anything; do not let them eat or drink. 8But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth. Let everyone call urgently on God. Let them give up their evil ways and their violence. 9Who knows? God may yet relent and with compassion turn from his fierce anger so that we will not perish."

10When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened.

elements.text: As you re-read, notice what Jonah’s motivation changes about the chapter — and watch the Ninevites’ response with fresh eyes, comparing it carefully with Jonah’s response in chapter 2.

elements.question: Picture Jonah standing on that beach, fresh out of the fish. He is grateful to be alive — but he still wants Nineveh destroyed. What do you think is going through his mind as he hears the word of the Lord come to him a second time?

elements.text: What Lessons Did Jonah Learn? (3:1–4)

elements.passageText: 1Then the word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and proclaim to it the message I give you." 3Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh. Now Nineveh was a very important city — a visit required three days. 4On the first day, Jonah started into the city. He proclaimed: "Forty more days and Nineveh will be destroyed."

elements.question: Jonah obeys the second call and goes to Nineveh (v3). Given what we now know about his heart from chapter 4, what lesson does he seem to have learned from the fish — and what lesson does he seem not to have learned?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah has learned a negative lesson: it is foolish to rebel against God’s word. You cannot hear what God says and walk off in the opposite direction — because this God is relentless in pursuing his purposes. The fish made that abundantly clear. So this time, Jonah does not run to Tarshish. He goes to Nineveh. But that is where the lesson ends. He has not learned to love what God loves, or to want what God wants. His heart is still set against mercy for his enemies. He obeys because he has calculated that he has no real alternative — perhaps hoping the Ninevites will just ignore him and be destroyed anyway. The fish produced compliance, not transformation. And the book of Jonah is very honest about that distinction.
elements.questionHint: The goDeeperData suggests Jonah may have been either ‘pessimistic’ (afraid the Ninevites would repent) or ‘optimistic’ (hoping they would ignore him). Either way, what had actually changed — and what had not?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: There is a difference between learning ‘I cannot get away with disobedience’ and learning ‘I want what God wants.’ Have you ever changed your behaviour because you had no alternative rather than because your heart had changed? What does Jonah’s case suggest about which kind of change God is ultimately after?

elements.term: God’s Word Must Be Reckoned With
elements.body: That is how it was for Jonah; that is how it is for us. God has not just spoken through Jonah — he has spoken through the gospel of Jesus Christ. Throughout the Old Testament, God spoke in many parts and many ways. But he has spoken his definitive word in his Son (Hebrews 1:1–3). Think of the word Jonah is given to preach: the warning is clear, but the message is not yet complete. Jesus makes the warning and hope of the gospel even more clear and complete — and it all turns on how we respond to him: ‘Whoever believes in the Son has eternal life, but whoever rejects the Son will not see life, for God’s wrath remains on him.’ (John 3:36) We cannot walk away and think there will be no comeback, any more than Jonah could. We stand in a more privileged position — so we have even less excuse if we refuse God’s word to us in Jesus.

elements.question: Jonah’s message is ‘Forty more days and Nineveh will be destroyed’ — a word of judgment, pure and simple. The goDeeperData notes that in every major message of judgment in the Bible — Noah, Sodom, the Exodus, the Exile — there is always a space, a delay, a time that passes. Why? What is that space for?
elements.sampleAnswer: The space is there for a purpose: will the hearers respond? God does not announce judgment and then bring it immediately. He announces it and waits — because his announcement is itself an act of grace, a window of opportunity. The forty days is not a countdown to inevitable destruction; it is forty days in which Nineveh can respond. This pattern runs throughout the Bible: God’s warnings of judgment carry within them an implicit invitation. If God simply wanted to destroy, he would not warn. The fact that he warns — through Noah, through Jonah, through his Son — is itself evidence that his heart is for people to turn and live. The judgment is real; but so is the grace that announces it in advance.
elements.questionHint: Why would God bother warning at all, if judgment were simply inevitable? What does the existence of the warning tell us about God’s purposes?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData describes God’s judgment as his ‘Yes’ to everything good and ‘No’ to everything evil. Why is that actually good news — and why does the gospel require that ‘No’ before it can speak its ‘Yes’?

elements.body: God’s judgment means him saying ‘Yes’ to everything good, and ‘No’ to everything evil.

elements.text: A Tale of One City (3:5–9)

elements.passageText: 5The Ninevites believed God. They declared a fast, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth. 6When the news reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his royal robes, covered himself with sackcloth and sat down in the dust. 7Then he issued a proclamation in Nineveh: "By the decree of the king and his nobles: Do not let any man or beast, herd or flock, taste anything; do not let them eat or drink. 8But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth. Let everyone call urgently on God. Let them give up their evil ways and their violence. 9Who knows? God may yet relent and with compassion turn from his fierce anger so that we will not perish."

elements.question: The Ninevites were violent, bloodthirsty pagans — Jonah was desperately hoping they would scorn his message. What actually happens, and what does the speed and totality of their response tell us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s worst fears come true: the city repents from top to bottom. He cannot even rely on the normal arrogance of kings — the king himself takes off his robes, gets off his throne, puts on sackcloth, and sits in the dust. The speed is striking: it happens immediately, from the greatest to the least. The totality is striking: not some of the city, not the religious ones — all of them. This mirrors what happened with the sailors in chapter 1 — rough pagans who knew nothing of the true God, but when confronted with the reality of his judgment, they feared, they believed, they turned. The pattern is consistent: when God’s word goes out, it has power. It does not return empty. And the people you would least expect to respond are sometimes the ones who do.
elements.questionHint: The Ninevites are described as the kind of people Jonah wanted destroyed — brutal and violent. What does their response say about who is capable of repentance?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: This is a re-run on a huge scale of the sailors’ story in chapter 1. In both cases, rough pagans respond to God more wholeheartedly than the prophet. What pattern is the book of Jonah establishing — and why might that pattern be uncomfortable for the religious reader?

elements.text: Clear-Cut Repentance: Nineveh vs Jonah (3:5–10)

elements.body: The Ninevites’ repentance in chapter 3 is genuine and unambiguous. But now that we know how the story ends, we need to compare it carefully with Jonah’s ‘repentance’ in chapter 2. The comparison is illuminating — and challenging.

elements.question: The goDeeperData draws a careful comparison between Jonah’s response in chapter 2 and the Ninevites’ response in chapter 3. Work through the comparison below and discuss what it reveals: • Jonah recognises he is in distress (2:2). The Ninevites recognise their ways are evil (3:8). What is the difference? • Jonah acknowledges that God has acted against him (2:3), but doesn’t comment on whether God was right. The Ninevites recognise that God’s fierce anger is justified in view of their evil (3:9). What does that add? • Jonah calls out for ‘help’ — deliverance from his circumstances. The Ninevites call out for mercy from God himself. What is the crucial difference?
elements.sampleAnswer: The differences are pointed and cumulative. Jonah recognised distress — he was in trouble and wanted out. The Ninevites went further: they named their evil ways specifically as evil, not just as a problem they were in trouble because of. Jonah acknowledged God had acted against him but never really admitted God was right to do so. The Ninevites, by contrast, implicitly accepted that God’s fierce anger was justified — they deserved what was coming. Most crucially, Jonah wanted deliverance from his circumstances. The Ninevites understood they needed something different: mercy from God himself. They needed to be delivered not just from the storm or the danger, but from God’s righteous anger. That is a much more searching understanding of the problem. And there is one more difference: the Ninevites actually turned from their evil ways (3:10). Jonah made promises of thanksgiving but never resolved to turn from anything.
elements.questionHint: Try to pinpoint the most important difference in the three comparisons. Which one goes deepest — and why?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The Ninevites understood they needed mercy from God himself — not just rescue from dangerous circumstances. Do you understand your own need in those terms? What is the difference between wanting God to fix your situation and genuinely seeking his mercy?

elements.text: Remorse Is Not Repentance

elements.body: Suppose you catch a train without buying a ticket, get caught, and have to pay a penalty fare. You are likely to feel remorse — regret that you were caught, regret at the fine, regret at the embarrassment. You might resolve never to get caught doing that again. But none of this is repentance. You could feel all of that and still feel hard done by, still feel resentful towards the inspector. Repentance would mean recognising it was right for you to be caught and punished. It would mean admitting the fare-dodging was wrong, and that you were wrong to do it. It would mean resolving to turn away from it, calling out to God for mercy and forgiveness, and actually doing the turning.
Regret that you got caught is not repentance. Sorrow about consequences is not repentance. Repentance means recognising you were wrong — not just unlucky — and turning back to God.

elements.question: Using the distinction between remorse and repentance, how would you describe Jonah’s response in chapter 2? And how does the Ninevites’ response in chapter 3 compare?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s response in chapter 2 looks more like remorse than repentance. He is in a desperate situation and wants out. He prays, he promises thanksgiving, he acknowledges God’s power. But he never names what he did as wrong. He never resolves to turn from his hatred of the Ninevites. He is sorry for his circumstances — not for his heart. The Ninevites’ response in chapter 3 is unambiguously repentance. They name their ways as evil. They accept that God’s anger is justified. They call out for mercy rather than just rescue. And crucially, they actually turn from their evil ways — God himself observes this (3:10). The pagans manage clear-cut repentance. The prophet manages something much less certain.
elements.questionHint: Jonah felt distress and called out to God. The Ninevites felt distress and called out to God. What was different about what each of them was actually asking for?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Think about your own experience of repentance. Have there been times when what you called repentance was actually closer to remorse — sorrow about being caught or about consequences, without a genuine turning? What does true repentance look like in practice for you?

elements.body: Repentance is a possibility for everyone — in fact, it is a demand for everyone. It is not optional. One of the things we learn from Nineveh — brutal, violent, pagan city that it was — is that repentance is possible for everybody. Not just religious types or irreligious types; not just respectable types or despicable types — everybody. There is no one so far gone that they cannot turn to God.
What have you repented of recently?

elements.question: The goDeeperData draws out four elements of genuine repentance from the Ninevites’ response: recognising our own wrongdoing; recognising we deserve God’s wrath; calling out for mercy; and actually turning away from wrongdoing. Which of these four is the hardest for you — and why?
elements.sampleAnswer: Each of the four has a common stumbling block. Recognising our wrongdoing is hard because we prefer to explain, justify, or minimise. Recognising we deserve God’s wrath is hard because it feels extreme — most of us do not feel that our sin is as serious as all that. Calling out for mercy is hard because it requires humility: we are asking for something we cannot earn or deserve. And actually turning away — not just resolving to, but doing it — is hard because habits of sin are deeply entrenched. The answer will be personal, and the question is designed to move from general understanding to specific self-examination.
elements.questionHint: Be honest about which step feels most distant. Is it naming what you have done as wrong? Accepting God’s anger is justified? Asking for mercy? Actually changing?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The Ninevites repented without certainty: ‘Who knows? God may yet relent.’ We, on the other side of the cross, have far more certainty — God will certainly respond with mercy to those who genuinely turn to him. Does that greater certainty make repentance easier or harder? And why might greater knowledge of grace sometimes make us less urgent about repentance?

elements.text: God’s Response — and a Question for Next Time (3:10)

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened.

elements.question: V10 says God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways. He did not simply hear their words or observe their sackcloth — he saw the turning. What does this tell us about what God is looking for when we repent?
elements.sampleAnswer: God sees past the outward signs — the fasting, the sackcloth, the proclamation — to the actual turning. He is not impressed by religious performance or elaborate expressions of sorrow. He is looking for the thing the outward signs are meant to represent: a genuine change of direction. The Ninevites’ outward actions were real, but what God noticed was the inward movement — they actually turned from their evil ways. This is a consistent theme in the prophets: God is not after ritual but reality, not ceremony but a changed life. The sackcloth mattered because it was genuine. God’s compassion was extended because the turning was real.
elements.questionHint: God ‘saw what they did’ and ‘how they turned.’ The emphasis is on what he observed, not just what they said. What is the difference between performing repentance and actually repenting?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: As chapter 3 closes, Jonah is still east of the city, watching. The goDeeperData notes that he may still be clinging to some hope that Nineveh will be destroyed after all. And it raises the question: what actually happened inside the fish? Did Jonah repent — or only feel remorse? What do you think, based on the evidence so far?

elements.body: As chapter 3 finishes, Jonah is east of the city, waiting. He knows God is interested in Nineveh, and it has repented. Maybe there is still a chance it will be destroyed. We cannot be sure what is in his mind. But chapter 4 has already told us his heart was never changed. So what exactly happened inside the fish? Did Jonah repent or not? Next session we rewind further — to chapter 2 — to find out.

elements.text: Reflection

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>The Ninevites believed God. They declared a fast, and all of them, from the greatest to the least, put on sackcloth.

elements.question: Repentance is not optional — and it is something we need to keep coming back to because sin is something we keep sliding back to. What have you repented of recently? And is there something you know you need to repent of now — not just feel sorry about, but actually turn from?
elements.sampleAnswer: The question is deliberately specific. Vague, general confession (‘I know I am a sinner’) is not the same as the concrete, named repentance that the Ninevites modelled. They knew what their evil ways were. They named them. They turned from them. The invitation here is to do the same — to bring something specific before God, acknowledge it as wrong, ask for his mercy, and take a concrete step away from it. This side of heaven, repentance is not a one-time event. It is a pattern of life.
elements.questionHint: Try to be as specific as you can. Remorse stays vague; repentance names the thing. What is the specific thing?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData raises repentance in three directions: for yourself, for the people around you, and for your city. Which of those three most needs your attention right now — and what would acting on it look like?

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>When God saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways, he had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened.

elements.question: God saw how the Ninevites turned from their evil ways. He was looking for the real thing, not the performance. Is there an area of your life where you have been performing repentance — going through the motions of sorrow or religious observance — without the actual turning? What would the real thing look like?
elements.sampleAnswer: Performing repentance is more common than we might like to admit. It can look like: confessing the same sin repeatedly without taking any steps to change; expressing sorrow in a group setting without any private honesty before God; or going through liturgical forms of confession without genuine engagement. None of those things are wrong in themselves — the danger is when they become substitutes for the actual turning. God sees what they did and how they turned. The ‘how’ matters. The actual movement matters. What would it look like to move from performance to the real thing in the area you have been sitting with?
elements.questionHint: The sackcloth and fasting were real — but what God noticed was the turning. What is the equivalent of the turning in the area you are thinking about?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: We know from chapter 4 that Jonah’s prayer in chapter 2 did not produce genuine repentance. How do we guard against the same failure in ourselves — praying sincerely without our hearts really changing? What role does the community of the church play in that?

elements.passageText: 1Then the word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and proclaim to it the message I give you." 3Jonah obeyed the word of the Lord and went to Nineveh. Now Nineveh was a very important city — a visit required three days. 4

elements.question: Jonah obeyed because he had no alternative — not because he had been transformed. The fish produced compliance, not a changed heart. Think about your own obedience to God. Is there an area where you are doing the right thing mainly because you feel you have no choice — rather than because you genuinely want what God wants? What would it look like to bring that gap honestly before God?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s compliance without transformation is uncomfortable precisely because it is so recognisable. We can give, serve, pray, and attend — all without our hearts being in it. We can obey because we fear the consequences of not obeying, or because we cannot see a way out, rather than because we genuinely love what God loves. The gap is not something to be ashamed of in the sense of hiding it. It is something to bring to God honestly: ‘Lord, I am doing this — but I am not sure I want to. Please change my heart, not just my behaviour.’ That is a prayer God can work with. Jonah’s mistake was not having wrong desires; it was refusing to bring them to God and ask him to change them.
elements.questionHint: The goal is not to feel guilty about joyless obedience, but to bring it honestly to God. What would one honest prayer about this look like this week?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: As we close session 5 and look toward session 6, we know we are going to re-examine Jonah’s prayer in the fish. Based on everything you have studied so far, what do you expect to find when we look more closely at chapter 2 — and what are you hoping to learn from it?

elements.text: What exactly is repentance? That is the question this session forces us to ask — especially now. Because at first glance it really does look as though Jonah turns over a new leaf in chapter 2. He prays. He thanks God. He resolves to make good on his vows. But we know from chapter 4 that it was not such a new leaf after all. His heart never changed. He still wanted Nineveh destroyed. So what happened in the fish? And what is the difference between what Jonah does in chapter 2 and genuine repentance?

elements.question: Re-read Jonah 2 before you begin — this time looking for what is missing, not just what is there…
elements.answer:
1From inside the fish Jonah prayed to the Lord his God. 2He said:

"In my distress I called to the Lord, and he answered me. From the depths of the grave I called for help, and you listened to my cry. 3You hurled me into the deep, into the very heart of the seas, and the currents swirled about me; all your waves and breakers swept over me. 4I said, ‘I have been banished from your sight; yet I will look again towards your holy temple.’ 5The engulfing waters threatened me, the deep surrounded me; seaweed was wrapped around my head. 6To the roots of the mountains I sank down; the earth beneath barred me in for ever. But you, Lord my God, brought my life up from the pit.

7When my life was ebbing away, I remembered you, Lord, and my prayer rose to you, to your holy temple. 8Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs. 9But I, with a song of thanksgiving, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. Salvation comes from the Lord.’"

10And the Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

elements.text: As you re-read, ask yourself: what would you expect to find in a genuine prayer of repentance from someone who has just done what Jonah has done? Then notice whether you find it.

elements.question: On your first reading of chapter 2 (back in session 2), did you assume Jonah had genuinely repented? What made it seem that way? And what does knowing chapter 4 change about how you read it now?

elements.text: Godly Sorrow and Worldly Sorrow (2 Corinthians 7:10)

elements.passageText: "Godly sorrow brings repentance that leads to salvation and leaves no regret, but worldly sorrow brings death." (2 Corinthians 7:10)

elements.question: 2 Corinthians 7:10 distinguishes godly sorrow from worldly sorrow. In your own words, what is the difference? And why is the distinction important for reading Jonah 2?
elements.sampleAnswer: Godly sorrow leads to genuine repentance — a change of direction — and leaves no regret. Worldly sorrow is distress about consequences: feeling bad about being in trouble, wanting the trouble to go away, perhaps even crying out to God for help — but without any real turning from sin. Paul says worldly sorrow brings death: it has the emotional texture of repentance without the substance of it. The distinction matters enormously for reading Jonah 2 because the chapter contains real sorrow, real prayer, real thankfulness — and yet chapter 4 shows us that something was missing. The question is which category Jonah’s sorrow falls into: godly sorrow that produces genuine change, or worldly sorrow that is essentially distress management.
elements.questionHint: Both kinds of sorrow can involve crying out to God for help and even feeling thankful when things improve. What is the thing that only godly sorrow produces — and that Jonah’s sorrow conspicuously does not?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Can you think of a time in your own life when you confused worldly sorrow with genuine repentance — when you felt bad about consequences without really turning from the sin itself? What eventually revealed the difference?

elements.body: Both kinds of sorrow can make someone cry out to God. Both can feel thankful when things improve. The difference is what happens next. Godly sorrow leads to genuine change — a turning from sin, not just a rescue from trouble. Worldly sorrow is essentially distress management: it wants relief, not transformation.

elements.text: What Is Missing from Jonah’s Prayer? (Jonah 2 and Psalm 51)

elements.body: Psalm 51 is probably the most famous prayer of repentance in the Bible. Read it alongside Jonah’s prayer in chapter 2 and look carefully for what is different. Pay particular attention to how each prayer talks about sin.

elements.passageText: Have mercy on me, O God, according to your unfailing love; according to your great compassion blot out my transgressions. Wash away all my iniquity and cleanse me from my sin. For I know my transgressions, and my sin is always before me. Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight, so that you are proved right when you speak and justified when you judge… Create in me a pure heart, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me… The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise. (Psalm 51:1–4, 10, 17)

elements.question: Compare Psalm 51 with Jonah’s prayer. What does David say about sin, guilt, and forgiveness that is completely absent from Jonah’s prayer in chapter 2?
elements.sampleAnswer: David’s prayer is saturated with the language of sin, guilt, and forgiveness. He names his transgressions specifically. He asks to be cleansed from his iniquity. He declares that God is right to judge him. He knows his sin is always before him. He asks for a clean heart and a renewed spirit. None of that is in Jonah’s prayer. Jonah’s chapter 2 is full of distress, rescue, and thankfulness — but there is no confession of sin, no acknowledgment of guilt, no asking for forgiveness, no resolve to turn from anything. He describes what happened to him; he does not name what he did. His prayer is about his situation, not his sin. That absence is the clearest evidence that what is happening in chapter 2 is worldly sorrow, not godly sorrow.
elements.questionHint: Read through Jonah 2 carefully. Can you find a single verse where Jonah names his sin, acknowledges his guilt, or asks for forgiveness? Compare that with the first four verses of Psalm 51.

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: David writes in Psalm 51:4 ‘Against you, you only, have I sinned.’ He had wronged Bathsheba and Uriah terribly — so why does he say ‘you only’? What does this tell us about the nature of all sin at its deepest level?

elements.term: No sin, no guilt, no forgiveness
elements.body: Jonah’s prayer in chapter 2 contains plenty of distress and plenty of thankfulness. But there is no confession of sin, no acknowledgment of guilt, no asking for forgiveness, no resolve to turn from anything. There is no repentance. So far, this is nothing but remorse and distress. Religious distress — but distress all the same.

elements.passageText: 1From inside the fish Jonah prayed to the Lord his God. 2He said: "In my distress I called to the Lord, and he answered me. From the depths of the grave I called for help, and you listened to my cry. 3You hurled me into the deep, into the very heart of the seas, and the currents swirled about me; all your waves and breakers swept over me. 4I said, ‘I have been banished from your sight; yet I will look again towards your holy temple.’ 5The engulfing waters threatened me, the deep surrounded me; seaweed was wrapped around my head. 6To the roots of the mountains I sank down; the earth beneath barred me in for ever. But you, Lord my God, brought my life up from the pit. 7When my life was ebbing away, I remembered you, Lord, and my prayer rose to you, to your holy temple."

elements.question: In session 2 we said Jonah’s prayer showed three marks of sorrow over sin: realising there is a problem, crying out for help, and thanking God. We now know those three things can characterise either godly sorrow or worldly sorrow. What would have needed to be different about Jonah’s prayer for it to be genuine repentance?
elements.sampleAnswer: Genuine repentance would have included Jonah naming what he actually did: deliberately running from God’s call because he did not want mercy shown to his enemies. It would have involved acknowledging that God was right to send the storm, right to have him thrown overboard. It would have involved asking for forgiveness, not just rescue. And it would have involved a genuine resolve to turn — not just to go to Nineveh because there was no alternative, but to want for Nineveh what God wants. None of that appears. Jonah describes his circumstances accurately and thanks God for his rescue. But he never turns the spotlight on himself and asks whether he was wrong. That is the missing ingredient.
elements.questionHint: Think about what Jonah actually did — the specific sin of chapter 1. Does he name it? Does he say it was wrong? Does he ask for forgiveness for it? Does he resolve to change his attitude toward Nineveh?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData makes the point that ‘any sorrow can recognise there is a problem — but if I am to repent, I need to recognise there is a problem with me.’ What is the difference between recognising you are in trouble and recognising you are in the wrong? And why is the second much harder?

elements.text: Jonah’s Blindness (2:8–9)

elements.passageText: "8Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs. 9But I, with a song of thanksgiving, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. Salvation comes from the Lord.’"

elements.question: V8 says ‘those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs.’ In the abstract, this is true. But in context, why is it deeply ironic — almost breathtakingly self-unaware?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah is in the belly of a fish, serving a self-inflicted sentence for running away from God, because he refused to warn the Ninevites to turn from their idols. He barely said a word to the sailors in chapter 1 — the very people who were in the middle of crying out to their various gods. And yet here he is, from inside the fish, pronouncing on the spiritual failure of people who cling to worthless idols. He is the expert. He has seen it. He has spotted the problem. The irony, of course, is that he cannot see the same problem in himself. He is running from God while lecturing about running from God. He is forfeiting grace while warning about forfeiting grace. The goDeeperData compares this to the Pharisee in Luke 18: ‘Thank you Lord that I am not like other sinners.’ It is the classic posture of someone who is unrepentant — they can see the speck in others’ eyes while missing the plank in their own.
elements.questionHint: Who was clinging to worthless idols in chapter 1? And who was supposed to be warning them about it, but instead ran away? Now Jonah preaches about it from inside the fish. What does that tell us about the state of his self-awareness?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Read Luke 18:9–14. In what ways does the Pharisee’s prayer resemble Jonah’s? What does Jesus say about the person who prays like that — and what does he say about the one who prays very differently?

elements.body: Are you applying Jonah to others rather than to yourself? If you find yourself thinking ‘so-and-so needs to hear this’ — be careful. Maybe they do. But the first question is always: do I need to hear this?

elements.question: V9 contains what the goDeeperData calls ‘probably the best bit of Jonah’s whole prayer’: ‘But I, with a song of thanksgiving, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. Salvation comes from the Lord.’ That sounds excellent. But compare it with Psalm 51:16–17. What is the crucial difference — and why does it matter?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah promises a song of thanksgiving, sacrifice, and fulfilled vows. These are good things — religious activities that honour God. David in Psalm 51 says: ‘You do not delight in sacrifice, or I would bring it. The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise.’ The crucial difference is the heart behind the offering. David knows that God is not primarily interested in the performance of religious acts. He wants the broken and contrite heart that the acts are supposed to express. Jonah offers the acts without the broken heart. If our heart is not right with God, the goDeeperData warns, God is not impressed by our religious activity, however sincere it may feel. Grand sacrifices of praise, devout prayers, earnest vows — God does not want our busyness. He wants our hearts.
elements.questionHint: What does Psalm 51:16–17 say God actually wants — and how does that compare with what Jonah is offering in v9?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData makes a sobering point: God can bring good out of our hypocrisy — he does it with Jonah in chapter 3. But if he does, he does it in spite of us, not because of us. How should that truth shape the way we think about our own Christian activity — our serving, giving, and witness?

elements.body: If we start to correct others from God’s word without first taking to heart God’s words of correction to us, we are starting to become a Jonah, a Pharisee, a hypocrite. God may be gracious enough to give us a second, third, fourth, or fifth chance. But practise turning down God’s chances to repent, and you might get rather good at it. Do not do that.
What have you learned recently from God’s word? Have you put it into practice?

elements.text: Mercy, Judgment, and the Thing About Fish (Jonah 2:10)

elements.passageText: 10And the Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

elements.question: When Jonah is swallowed by the fish, is that an act of mercy or judgment? The goDeeperData suggests you can make a reasonable case both ways. What is the case for each — and how does holding both together help us understand what God is doing?
elements.sampleAnswer: The case for mercy: without the fish, Jonah would have drowned. God uses the fish to preserve his life, give him time to reflect, and eventually deposit him on dry land. Jonah’s own prayer in chapter 2 frames it largely as rescue. The case for judgment: being swallowed by a giant sea creature was not, to an ancient Israelite, a happy event. Sea monsters in the Bible are associated with chaos and destruction. It is God’s dramatic and severe response to Jonah’s stubborn rebellion. Jonah has been running so hard that God has to use something extreme to stop him. The goDeeperData’s resolution is that both are true simultaneously: this is what judgment means. God’s judgment is not simply condemnation or simply salvation — it is the process of saying ‘Yes’ to good and ‘No’ to evil, and both can happen in the same event, even on the same person.
elements.questionHint: Think about how a sea creature would have felt to an ancient Israelite reader. Then think about what the fish actually accomplishes. Can an event be both terrible and gracious at the same time?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData traces the pattern of judgment bringing both condemnation and salvation through the Flood (Noah delivered, others drowned) and the Exodus (Israel saved, Egypt condemned). Can you think of other examples in Scripture where salvation and judgment come together? What does the pattern tell us about how God works?

elements.body: If judgment was a coin, then salvation and condemnation would be the two sides of that one coin.

elements.text: The Sign of Jonah: Making Connections (Matthew 12:38–41)

elements.passageText: "Then some of the Pharisees and Scribes answered him, ‘Teacher, we want to see a sign from you.’ But he answered them, ‘An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign! And no sign will be given to it except the sign of Jonah the prophet. For just as Jonah was in the belly of the sea creature three days and three nights, so the Son of Man will be in the heart of the earth three days and three nights. The men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it — because they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and now one greater than Jonah is here.’" (Matthew 12:38–41)

elements.question: Jesus compares himself to Jonah — a prophet who falls far short of God’s standards. The goDeeperData notes that this is almost the only time Jesus compares himself to an Old Testament prophet. What is the crucial parallel that makes the comparison work — and what does it tell us about the cross?
elements.sampleAnswer: The crucial parallel is that both Jonah and Jesus experience God’s judgment of condemnation and God’s judgment of salvation on the same person, at the same time. Jonah undergoes God’s ‘No’ — three days in the belly of the sea creature — and then experiences God’s ‘Yes’ on the third day. Jesus undergoes God’s ‘No’ — three days in the heart of the earth — and then experiences God’s ‘Yes’ on the third day. But with Jesus, the stakes are infinitely higher: he has done nothing to deserve the ‘No.’ He takes it on behalf of others. Because he has faced God’s condemnation in our place, we can hear God’s ‘Yes’ of salvation. The cross is the place where wrath and mercy meet — where justice and deliverance are brought together on one person, so that we do not have to face them ourselves.
elements.questionHint: The goDeeperData says this combination — condemnation and salvation on the same person — only happens twice in the whole Bible. What makes it so unusual? And why does it matter enormously that it happens with Jesus?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData says: ‘Because he has faced God’s “No,” we can hear God’s “Yes.” He bears our punishment to win our peace. The cross is the place where wrath and mercy meet.’ What difference does it make that Jesus is not just an example to follow but someone who has taken God’s judgment in our place? How does that change the nature of repentance for us, compared with the Ninevites who did not know this?

elements.body: In all God’s purposes, his judgment involves condemnation and salvation. But in Jesus Christ, condemnation and salvation are focused together on one person. In him justice and deliverance are brought together. Because he has faced God’s ‘No,’ we can hear God’s ‘Yes.’ He bears our punishment to win our peace. The cross is the place where wrath and mercy meet.

elements.text: Reflection

elements.passageText: "Godly sorrow brings repentance that leads to salvation and leaves no regret, but worldly sorrow brings death." (2 Corinthians 7:10)

elements.question: Jonah felt genuine distress and prayed genuinely to God — but it was worldly sorrow, not godly sorrow. Is there an area of your life where you have been feeling bad about a situation or its consequences, but have not yet acknowledged the sin behind it or asked God for forgiveness? What would moving from worldly sorrow to godly sorrow look like there?
elements.sampleAnswer: Worldly sorrow is extremely common, and it can feel very much like repentance from the inside. The test is what it produces: does the sorrow lead to change, or does it lead back to the same pattern once the immediate pressure has eased? Moving from worldly sorrow to godly sorrow usually requires naming the specific sin — not just ‘I feel bad about this situation’ but ‘I did this specific wrong thing and it was wrong.’ It requires accepting that God was right to confront you with it. It requires asking specifically for forgiveness. And it requires taking a concrete step of turning — not just resolving to do better, but actually doing something different.
elements.questionHint: Try to name the thing specifically — not the situation, but the sin. Have you named it to God? Have you asked for forgiveness? Have you taken any concrete step of turning?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Paul says godly sorrow ‘leaves no regret.’ What does that mean — and what is the difference between the absence of regret that comes from genuine repentance and the absence of regret that comes from hardening your heart?

elements.passageText: "8Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs."

elements.question: Jonah could see the spiritual failure of the idol-cloggers around him while being completely blind to his own. Is there a pattern of sin in someone else’s life that you find it easy to see and comment on, while being less honest about the same or a related pattern in yourself? What would honest self-examination look like — starting with yourself?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah’s blindness is the blindness of someone who has been applying God’s word outward rather than inward. The goDeeperData does not say there is never a place for noticing others’ sins — the Bible calls us to carry one another’s burdens and exhort one another. But that must be secondary. Starting with honest self-examination, specifically in the area you are most tempted to project onto others, is the place to begin. It might be worth asking: the thing I most often notice and comment on in others — is there any version of that in me? What would it look like to bring that to God first, before I say anything to anyone else?
elements.questionHint: The question is not whether to notice others’ sins — it is about where to start. Where do you need to start today?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData warns that if we correct others from God’s word without first applying it to ourselves, we start to become ‘a Jonah, a Pharisee, a hypocrite.’ How do you build in habits of honest self-examination so that God’s word consistently reaches you before it reaches your commentary on others?

elements.passageText: ‘For just as Jonah was in the belly of the sea creature three days and three nights, so the Son of Man will be in the heart of the earth three days and three nights.’ (Matthew 12:40)

elements.question: Jesus faced God’s ‘No’ in our place so that we could hear God’s ‘Yes.’ The cross is the place where wrath and mercy meet. How does that truth — not just known but genuinely believed — change the way you approach repentance? Does knowing the cross is behind you make genuine repentance easier or more costly?
elements.sampleAnswer: The cross changes everything about repentance. For the Ninevites, repentance was a desperate gamble: ‘Who knows? God may yet relent.’ For those who know the cross, repentance is a homecoming: God has already acted to make forgiveness possible. The ‘No’ has already been spoken — at the cross, not over us. That means genuine repentance is not a frightening step toward an uncertain God but a confident step toward a God whose character has been fully revealed in Jesus. Knowing the cross might actually make genuine repentance harder in one sense: it removes every excuse for staying in worldly sorrow. If God has gone to that length to make forgiveness possible, the only right response is to take it seriously — to name the sin, receive the forgiveness, and actually turn.
elements.questionHint: The Ninevites repented without certainty. You have certainty. Does that certainty — the knowledge that the cross has dealt with sin — make you more eager to repent, or more comfortable staying where you are? What does your answer reveal?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Looking back over sessions 5 and 6 together: Jonah 3 showed us what clear-cut repentance looks like (the Ninevites). Jonah 2 has shown us what it does not look like (Jonah). And Matthew 12 has shown us the one in whom condemnation and salvation meet. What is the single most important thing you want to carry forward from these two sessions into your daily life?

chapters.chapterTitle: Don’t Say a Word: Jonah 1 Revisited

elements.text: We have reached the end of the backward journey. We know the whole story now — where Jonah ends up, what was in his heart all along, what kind of sorrow his prayer in the fish actually was. Now we return to where it all began: chapter 1. And like the final read-through of a murder mystery, when you already know who did it and why, the opening scenes look completely different.

elements.question: Re-read Jonah 1 one final time — now knowing everything the book has shown you…
elements.answer:
1The word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me."

3But Jonah ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish. He went down to Joppa, where he found a ship bound for that port. After paying the fare, he went aboard and sailed for Tarshish to flee from the Lord.

4Then the Lord sent a great wind on the sea, and such a violent storm arose that the ship threatened to break up. 5All the sailors were afraid and each cried out to his own god. And they threw the cargo into the sea to lighten the ship.

But Jonah had gone below deck, where he lay down and fell into a deep sleep. 6The captain went to him and said, "How can you sleep? Get up and call on your god! Maybe he will take notice of us so that we will not perish."

7Then the sailors said to each other, "Come, let us cast lots to find out who is responsible for this calamity." They cast lots and the lot fell on Jonah. 8So they asked him, "Tell us, who is responsible for making all this trouble for us? What kind of work do you do? Where do you come from? What is your country? From what people are you?"

9He answered, "I am a Hebrew and I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land."

10This terrified them and they asked, "What have you done?" (They knew he was running away from the Lord, because he had already told them so.)

11The sea was getting rougher and rougher. So they asked him, "What should we do to you to make the sea calm down for us?"

12"Pick me up and throw me into the sea," he replied, "and it will become calm. I know that it is my fault that this great storm has come upon you."

13Instead, the men did their best to row back to land. But they could not, for the sea grew even wilder than before. 14Then they cried out to the Lord, "Please, Lord, do not let us die for taking this man’s life. Do not hold us accountable for killing an innocent man, for you, Lord, have done as you pleased." 15Then they took Jonah and threw him overboard, and the raging sea grew calm. 16At this the men greatly feared the Lord, and they offered a sacrifice to the Lord and made vows to him.

17Now the Lord provided a huge fish to swallow Jonah, and Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.

elements.text: As you re-read, watch two things in particular: the contrast between Jonah’s ‘fear’ and the sailors’ fear; and the question of what happens when God’s word goes out — whether it can really be stopped.

elements.question: Having now read Jonah 1 three times across this study — the first time cold, then knowing how the story ends, and now knowing everything — what do you understand about the opening chapter that you did not understand when you first read it?

elements.text: Scene 1 — Call and Response (1:1–3)

elements.passageText: 1The word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai: 2"Go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me." 3But Jonah ran away from the Lord and headed for Tarshish. He went down to Joppa, where he found a ship bound for that port. After paying the fare, he went aboard and sailed for Tarshish to flee from the Lord.

elements.body: Jonah was already known as a prophet — a real, established figure who had spoken genuine words from God (2 Kings 14:25). His commission here is unique in the Old Testament: it is the only place where a prophet is called to preach to a pagan nation. But both the prophet and the commission are a mixture of familiar and unusual — which makes what happens next all the more striking.

elements.question: Compare the call of Elijah in 1 Kings 17:8–10 with the call of Jonah in 1:1–3. The pattern is almost identical — until v3. Now that you know why Jonah ran, how does the upside-down nature of his response look different from how it looked on a first read?
elements.sampleAnswer: On a first read, Jonah’s flight looks like fear or weakness — the sympathy naturally goes to him. Knowing chapter 4, it looks completely different. The standard prophetic pattern is: God speaks, prophet arises, prophet goes. Jonah follows the pattern up to a point: the word comes, Jonah arises. But then: Jonah heads in entirely the opposite direction — not from fear but from calculation. He does not want the Ninevites warned. He wants them dead. His silence is an act of malice, not cowardice. The upside-down start is not the stumbling of a frightened man; it is the deliberate rebellion of a bitter one. And that makes it a great deal worse.
elements.questionHint: The goDeeperData puts it starkly: Jonah runs away not because he is ‘too soft’ but because he is ‘too hard.’ What is the difference — and how does it change the moral character of his flight?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The call to Nineveh echoes the story of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 18:25): God is the judge of all the earth, and wickedness that ‘comes up before him’ will be addressed. How should that truth shape the way we think about our own cities and cultures — their apparent untouchability, and what they owe to the God who made them?

elements.body: One of the major themes and encouragements of Jonah is that God’s word will do its job. Even in the face of stubborn rebellion by those who ought to be on God’s side, his word still achieves his purposes. That is how powerful God’s word is. That is how powerful God is.

elements.text: Scene 2 — Storm and Response (1:4–6)

elements.passageText: 4Then the Lord sent a great wind on the sea, and such a violent storm arose that the ship threatened to break up. 5All the sailors were afraid and each cried out to his own god. And they threw the cargo into the sea to lighten the ship. But Jonah had gone below deck, where he lay down and fell into a deep sleep. 6The captain went to him and said, "How can you sleep? Get up and call on your god! Maybe he will take notice of us so that we will not perish."

elements.question: The goDeeperData notes that the storm produces ‘a whole range of ways people can respond to God’s judgment — fear, prayer, human effort of every kind.’ And then there is Jonah: comatose below deck. Now that you know what is driving Jonah, how does his sleep read differently from how it did in session 1?
elements.sampleAnswer: In session 1, Jonah’s sleep felt like extreme passivity or denial — the Ostrich Trick, burying his head. Knowing chapter 4, it reads as something more active and deliberate. Jonah is not simply overwhelmed; he is ‘totally desensitised,’ as the goDeeperData puts it, by his own rebellion. He does not want to call on the Lord — because calling on the Lord would mean engaging with a God whose purposes he is actively opposing. And engaging with those purposes might lead to repentance, which might lead back to Nineveh. So he sleeps. The sleep is not weakness; it is the numbness of someone who has closed themselves off from God so thoroughly that even a catastrophic storm cannot reach them.
elements.questionHint: The goDeeperData says Jonah has ‘about as much life and sensitivity as a block of wood.’ Is that because he cannot feel the storm — or because he has decided not to respond to it?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The captain tells Jonah to ‘arise’ and call on his god. This is the second time in the chapter Jonah has been told to ‘arise’ (cf. 1:2). In both cases he gets up but does not follow the instruction. What does the pattern of partial obedience tell us about the state of his heart? Where do you notice a similar pattern in yourself?

elements.text: Scene 3 — Fear and Worship (1:7–10)

elements.passageText: 8So they asked him, "Tell us, who is responsible for making all this trouble for us? What kind of work do you do? Where do you come from? What is your country? From what people are you?" 9He answered, "I am a Hebrew and I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land." 10This terrified them and they asked, "What have you done?" (They knew he was running away from the Lord, because he had already told them so.)

elements.question: Jonah says ‘I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.’ The goDeeperData says this ‘rings hollow.’ Now that you know everything about Jonah, what exactly is hollow about it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jonah uses the word translated ‘worship’ — literally ‘fear.’ In the Old Testament, to fear the Lord is the comprehensive description of what it means to live in right relationship with God: to revere him, trust him, obey him, order your life by his word. But Jonah has done none of those things in this chapter. He heard God’s word and deliberately ran in the opposite direction. He has not called on the Lord when the storm came. He is sitting below deck while a storm his own disobedience caused threatens to kill innocent sailors. His claim to ‘fear the Lord’ is entirely disconnected from his behaviour. It is the confession of a man who can articulate true theology while his life contradicts every word of it. The sailors, by contrast, have just learned about this God for the first time — and their response is immediate, visceral, and real.
elements.questionHint: The goDeeperData draws a sharp contrast: Jonah ‘says’ he fears the Lord; the sailors ‘actually’ fear the Lord. What is the difference between knowing the right words about God and actually fearing him?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData asks: ‘If someone asked you how you felt about God, would it occur to you to use the word “fear” in any context whatsoever?’ What is healthy about fearing God — and what does losing that fear do to how we read the Bible, how we pray, and how seriously we take his word?

elements.body:
Do you fear God?

This is also the God of tenderness, who stoops down and cares for each one of us — the God who takes on our frail flesh and blood and becomes one of us in the person of his Son, Jesus Christ. If we forget how fearfully awesome God is, we will take for granted how wonderfully precious it is to be his children.

elements.question: The sailors learn about Jonah’s God in v9 and immediately respond with ‘What have you done?!’ (v10). Even with no prior knowledge of the Lord, they grasp the seriousness of what Jonah has done better than Jonah himself seems to. What does that tell us about the relationship between knowledge of God and appropriate response to him?
elements.sampleAnswer: The sailors have one moment’s glimpse of who God is, and they respond with appropriate alarm. Jonah has a lifetime of knowledge — and shows no such alarm at all. This runs through the whole book as a pattern: the sailors, the king of Nineveh, the whole city — everyone who encounters the truth about God responds to it. The one person who does not is the prophet himself. The lesson is not that knowledge of God is unimportant. It is that knowledge of God, when it is not held in living faith, can actually insulate against the response it should produce. Familiarity breeds not just contempt but a kind of numbness: Jonah has heard the Lord’s voice so many times that he has learned how to tune it out. The sailors, hearing it for the first time, cannot.
elements.questionHint: The sailors knew nothing about the Lord before v9. Jonah knew everything. Who responds more appropriately — and what does that suggest about the dangers of familiarity with God’s word?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Is it possible to become so familiar with God’s word that it stops having weight? How do you guard against that kind of spiritual numbness — the log-like slumber the goDeeperData describes?

elements.text: Scene 4 — He Would Rather Die (1:11–16)

elements.passageText: 11The sea was getting rougher and rougher. So they asked him, "What should we do to you to make the sea calm down for us?" 12"Pick me up and throw me into the sea," he replied, "and it will become calm. I know that it is my fault that this great storm has come upon you." 13Instead, the men did their best to row back to land. But they could not, for the sea grew even wilder than before. 14Then they cried out to the Lord, "Please, Lord, do not let us die for taking this man’s life. Do not hold us accountable for killing an innocent man, for you, Lord, have done as you pleased." 15Then they took Jonah and threw him overboard, and the raging sea grew calm. 16At this the men greatly feared the Lord, and they offered a sacrifice to the Lord and made vows to him.

elements.question: The goDeeperData says there is no indication in the text that God told Jonah to instruct the sailors to throw him overboard — ‘the truth is probably much bleaker — it was Jonah’s own idea.’ He would rather drown than go back to God and risk the Ninevites being saved. How does knowing that change your reading of his instruction in v12?
elements.sampleAnswer: On a first reading, v12 can look heroic — Jonah sacrificing himself to save the sailors, taking responsibility for the storm. Knowing chapter 4, it looks very different. Jonah is not choosing death as an act of noble self-sacrifice. He is choosing death as the final act of his rebellion: he would rather die than repent, and would rather die than give the Ninevites any chance of hearing God’s word and being spared. His own death is preferable to their salvation. This is how deeply the hatred has gone. The sailors, who have no such motive, do everything they can to avoid throwing him over. The contrast is the final, sharpest irony of the chapter: the pagans fight to save the prophet’s life; the prophet is willing to throw it away rather than do what God asks.
elements.questionHint: In session 1, we gave Jonah credit for taking responsibility for the storm. Does knowing his motive change whether that credit is deserved?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Jonah’s rebellion has reached the point where he prefers death to obedience. The goDeeperData traces a progression: Jonah the rebel, the runner, the slumberer, and finally the one who would rather die than submit. What does this progression tell us about where persistent refusal to heed God’s word leads?

elements.question: V16 says the sailors ‘greatly feared the Lord’ and offered sacrifice and made vows. The goDeeperData makes the pointed observation: ‘Jonah says he fears the Lord; the sailors actually fear the Lord. Jonah’s “fear” rings hollow; their fear is real and godly.’ What does genuine fear of the Lord produce in the sailors that Jonah’s claimed fear has conspicuously failed to produce in him?
elements.sampleAnswer: The sailors’ fear produces exactly what fear of the Lord is supposed to produce: worship. They offer sacrifice. They make vows. They turn from their own gods to the true God. Their entire relationship with the divine is reoriented by what they have witnessed. Jonah’s claimed fear has produced none of that. It has not produced obedience to God’s word. It has not produced prayer in the storm. It has not produced concern for the sailors. It has not produced any move toward repentance. Genuine fear of God, the goDeeperData notes, is what we call worship — it is the full orientation of a life toward the God who is fearfully awesome. What Jonah has is mere theological familiarity: he knows the right words, but they have not reached his life.
elements.questionHint: The sailors go from pagans crying to their own gods, to worshippers of the Lord making vows — all in one chapter. What does their transformation reveal about what genuine fear of God looks like in practice?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The sailors’ conversion is driven not by a sermon but by witnessing God’s power and Jonah’s exposure. What does that tell us about the relationship between the power of God’s word, the character of his messengers, and the spreading of the gospel? And what is the lesson for us?

elements.text: When God Speaks — Two Kinds of Response

elements.question: The goDeeperData ends with a stark binary: when we hear God’s word, we either rebel (and end up in log-like slumber) or we heed (and receive life-giving mercy). Looking back over the whole book of Jonah, what evidence have you seen of both responses — and in whom?
elements.sampleAnswer: The book is full of both responses, and the distribution is surprising. Heed: the sailors (ch1), the Ninevites (ch3), and even the king of Nineveh — all respond to God’s word with immediate, total, genuine turning. Rebel: Jonah — consistently, across the whole book. He hears God’s word and runs (ch1). He prays in extremity but without genuine repentance (ch2). He obeys mechanically without his heart (ch3). He rages at the outcome (ch4). The book’s diagnosis: the log-like slumber is not the state of pagans who have never heard. It is the state of someone who has heard many times and chosen, each time, not to heed. The gospel is powerful enough to reach the most violent pagan city in the ancient world. The same gospel cannot seem to reach the heart of the prophet who carries it.
elements.questionHint: Think through every character in the book: Jonah, the sailors, the captain, the Ninevites, the king. Which ones respond with heeding, and which with rebellion? Is the pattern what you expected?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData connects this to Hebrews 1:1–3: God has spoken supremely in his Son. If the people of Nineveh will stand at the judgment and condemn those who had more than they did (Matthew 12:41), what does that mean for us — who have the full gospel of Jesus Christ?

elements.body: Whenever we hear the word of God we face a simple choice. We may respond in a thousand different ways. But regardless of the differences, all those ways will fit into one of two stark types: we rebel, and end up with log-like slumber; or we heed, and receive life-giving mercy. Everyone has to pick one or the other. Let’s pick wisely — let’s heed.

elements.text: Looking Back at the Whole Journey

elements.question: We have now travelled through the book of Jonah twice — forwards and backwards. What is the single biggest thing the book has changed about the way you think about God, about yourself, or about the people around you?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is an open question designed to gather the fruit of the whole study. Common answers might include: a more honest recognition of Jonah-like attitudes in themselves; a greater sense of God’s sovereignty over every detail of life; a deeper understanding of the difference between remorse and genuine repentance; a changed attitude toward a particular person or group they had written off; or a renewed sense of the power and seriousness of God’s word. Encourage specificity: not ‘I learned a lot’ but ‘this specific thing changed’.
elements.questionHint: Try to name one thing specifically — not a general impression but a concrete shift in understanding, attitude, or intention.

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The goDeeperData closes with the image of God’s word going out and not being thwarted — not even by a prophet actively working against it. How does that truth change the way you think about sharing the gospel with people around you — especially those who seem most unlikely to respond?

elements.text: Reflection

elements.passageText: He answered, "I am a Hebrew and I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land."

elements.question: Jonah could say the right things about God while his life contradicted them entirely. Is there a gap between what you say — or know — about God and how you are actually living? What is the most significant area where your theology and your behaviour are currently out of joint?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is Jonah’s defining failure: the gap between his confession and his life. He worships the God of heaven who made the sea and the dry land — on a boat, on the sea, running from that God. The gap between what we profess and how we live is one of the most persistent features of human sinfulness, and Jonah is its most vivid case study. The question invites honest self-examination: not ‘do I know the right things?’ but ‘are those things shaping how I actually live?’ What area of life is currently furthest from alignment with what you believe?
elements.questionHint: Try to be specific. Not ‘I could pray more’ but a concrete area — a relationship, a habit, a pattern of thought or speech — where what you believe and what you do are furthest apart.

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: What would it take to close that gap? Not in general — but this specific gap, this week. What is one concrete step?

elements.passageText: 15Then they took Jonah and threw him overboard, and the raging sea grew calm. 16At this the men greatly feared the Lord, and they offered a sacrifice to the Lord and made vows to him.

elements.question: The sailors feared the Lord genuinely — and it produced worship. Jonah claimed to fear the Lord — and it produced nothing. What does genuine fear of God look like in your daily life? Not the anxiety of someone unsure of their standing before God — but the reverence of someone who knows how awesome he is and takes his word seriously because of it?
elements.sampleAnswer: The goDeeperData makes an important clarification: fearing God is not about anxiety or uncertainty about his love. It is the awe and reverence appropriate to someone who knows they are dealing with the God who rules the universe, commands the storm, and speaks words that cannot be thwarted. It produces a certain seriousness about his word: not taking it lightly, not filing it away without acting on it, not going through the motions. It also produces a certain tenderness — because the fearfully awesome God is also the God who became flesh for us. The combination of awe and intimacy is what the sailors begin to experience in v16, and what Jonah has lost.
elements.questionHint: Think about how you approach God in prayer, in reading Scripture, in worship. Is there a sense of awe — of dealing with someone truly immense — or has familiarity dulled that?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
