And that makes us reconsider: what exactly happened inside the fish? Did Jonah repent or not? We will have to rewind a bit further to find out.

images.alt: Assyrian relief showing a vulture carrying off intestines
images.caption: Image 6: A vulture carries off intestines. Intestines represented compassion, so the message is: ‘All who resist Assyria will be treated without mercy.’ British Museum, Accession no. WA 118907.

footnotes.body: This section particularly draws on John Woodhouse’s sermons on Jonah.
There is, of course, also the parallel passage in Luke 11:29–30. And perhaps the allusion to Elijah and Elisha in Luke 4:24–27 should count too.

opening.body: So what exactly is repentance? That is one question this story about Jonah keeps forcing us to ask — and we especially need to ask it as we move from Jonah 3 to revisit Jonah 2. Because at first glance it does look like Jonah has turned over a new leaf in chapter 2. But we know from Jonah 4 that it was not such a new leaf after all. So — what is repentance? How is it different from what we see in Jonah 2?

wholeNewLeaf.body: When we first looked at Jonah 2, we picked out three points to do with being sorry over sin: sorrow realises there is a problem; it cries out to God for help; and it thanks the God who saves. That might have seemed reasonable enough at the time. On the first read through, you probably wanted to assume the best of Jonah, and assume that he really was repentant.

benefitOfDoubt.body:
Normally it is good to begin by giving people the benefit of the doubt. But any doubt there was, any uncertainty — Jonah’s behaviour in chapter 4 has blown it right out of the water. There is no doubt any more: Jonah fundamentally has not repented. He does not want God’s love and grace to be shown to the Ninevites, and that gives us a pretty big hint that he probably has not understood the love and grace God has shown to him. So let’s use that knowledge — that something goes badly wrong further down the line — to see if we can spot any early signs of it here in chapter 2.

There is a verse in the New Testament that gives us a clue about what might be wrong:

> *’Godly sorrow brings repentance that leads to salvation and leaves no regret, but worldly sorrow brings death.’* (2 Corinthians 7:10)

godlySorrow.body:
On our first run through Jonah 2 we picked out three points to do with sorrow — and they are true, no matter what sort of sorrow we are talking about. ’Godly sorrow’ will realise there is a problem, cry out to God for help, and thank God for sorting it out. But — and this is the scary thing — ‘worldly sorrow’ could do some, or even all, of those as well.

Worldly sorrow (or remorse or fear or desperation) could well make someone cry out for help. In a desperate situation, even a godless person might see the problem and cry out to God for help. They might even feel thankful to God for a bit, and still go back to being just as godless afterwards.

Repentance is not the same as sorrow. Sometimes we make that mistake — we think repentance means feeling sorry or sad. But actually that is just remorse. And if that is all it is, 2 Corinthians would call it worldly sorrow. Repentance is not just about feeling sorrowful or sad or regretful. Repentance is about *change*. It means recognising that we have done wrong, and turning away from it. Repentance is about turning away from sin, and turning back to God. Any sorrow can recognise there is a problem — but if I am to repent, I need to recognise there is a problem *with me*.

psalm51Section.body: It will be easier to see what is missing from Jonah’s prayer in chapter 2 if we compare it to a genuine, unambiguous prayer of repentance. Psalm 51 is probably the best known and most striking repentance psalm in the Bible. As you read through it, look out for the differences between it and Jonah’s prayer — and in particular: what is different about the way this psalm talks about sin?

psalm51.body:
> Have mercy on me, O God, according to your unfailing love;
> according to your great compassion blot out my transgressions.
> Wash away all my iniquity and cleanse me from my sin.
>
> For I know my transgressions, and my sin is always before me.
> Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight,
> so that you are proved right when you speak and justified when you judge.
> Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me.
> Surely you desire truth in the inner parts; you teach me wisdom in the inmost place.
>
> Cleanse me with hyssop, and I shall be clean; wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow.
> Let me hear joy and gladness; let the bones you have crushed rejoice.
> Hide your face from my sins and blot out all my iniquity.
> Create in me a pure heart, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me.
> Do not cast me from your presence or take your Holy Spirit from me.
> Restore to me the joy of your salvation and grant me a willing spirit, to sustain me.
>
> Then I will teach transgressors your ways, and sinners will turn back to you.
> Save me from bloodguilt, O God, the God who saves me,
> and my tongue will sing of your righteousness.
> O Lord, open my lips, and my mouth will declare your praise.
> You do not delight in sacrifice, or I would bring it;
> you do not take pleasure in burnt offerings.
> The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit;
> a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise.
>
> In your good pleasure make Zion prosper; build up the walls of Jerusalem.
> Then there will be righteous sacrifices, whole burnt offerings to delight you;
> then bulls will be offered on your altar. *(Psalm 51:1–19)*

Did you see what was different? David is absolutely clear about his own guilt and transgression and sin. He knows he is in the wrong, and that he needs mercy and forgiveness.

callout4.body: No sin, no guilt, no forgiveness

noSin.body: That is completely missing from Jonah’s prayer. There is plenty of distress and remorse, there is plenty of crying out to God for help — there is even thankfulness. But there is no confession of sin, there is no acknowledgement of guilt, there is no asking for forgiveness. There is no repentance. So far, this is nothing but remorse and distress. It is religious distress, in that Jonah calls on God to get him out of danger and he thanks God. But even so, it is still just distress. And distress is not the same as repentance.

jonahsBlindness.body:
Consider verse 8: *’Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs.’* In the abstract, that is true. But in context it is bitingly ironic — because Jonah has refused to go to the Ninevites to warn them against their idols, and largely ignored the sailors of chapter 1, the very men who were busy turning away from their idols to give thanks to God.

There is an unhealthy dose of pride and blindness in what Jonah says here. He is a bit like the Pharisee in Jesus’ parable: ‘I thank you Lord that I am not like other sinners — especially those pagan sailors there. Look at them, clinging to their pathetic idols.’ (Luke 18:9–14.)

This is a classic sign of someone who is unrepentant and becoming hardened or blind. They can see the speck in their brother’s eye but cannot see the whopping big plank in their own. It is amazing how deceitful sin can be, and how blind it can make us to our own faults — even when we think we can see them clearly in others.

pft1.body:
Are you applying Jonah to you?

If you catch yourself thinking, ‘So-and-so needs to hear this’ — be careful. Maybe they do. But our primary responsibility, all the time, is to ask ourselves first: *’Do I need to hear this? Are there areas of my life that this applies to?’* That is where each of us should start.

whereToStart.body: Jonah did not start there, and that is part of where he got into trouble. There might have been a time and place for him to think about the sin of the sailors or the Ninevites. But that time was not while he himself was in the belly of a whale for having run away from God. The sailors are not in the whale. The Ninevites are not in the whale. Jonah is in the whale. A fair chunk of his thoughts should have been on whether his own conduct needed to change.

whereToStart2.body:
We each need to spot our own failings first — I need to spot mine; you need to spot yours. That is the place to start.

There is a place for spotting others’ failings too, so that we are not carried along with them and can spur one another to change. The Bible has a lot to say about that, and we could probably do with giving it more thought. We do need to carry one another’s burdens and exhort one another daily. Sometimes I will be more blind to my own sin and you will be able to see it more clearly, and vice versa. Sometimes we can help each other clear up our own blind spots.

dangerousRoad.body:
But that is secondary. If we start to correct others from God’s word without first taking to heart God’s words of correction to us, then we are starting to become a Jonah, a Pharisee, a hypocrite. That road leads to spiritual decay.

And that is deadly. Verse 9 contains probably the best bit of Jonah’s whole prayer: *’But I, with a song of thanksgiving, will sacrifice to you. What I have vowed I will make good. Salvation comes from the Lord.’* That sounds pretty good. But compare it with what David says in Psalm 51:

> *’You do not delight in sacrifice, or I would bring it; you do not take pleasure in burnt offerings. The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise.’* (51:16–17)

If our heart is not right with God, then God could not care less about our efforts to please him or work for him — whether we are trying to be some sort of super-prophet, super-missionary, or just doing our small part. If we are pressing ahead on a sinful track, God is not going to be the least impressed. It does not matter how grand our sacrifice of praise, how devout our prayers, how earnest our vows — God does not want our busyness. He wants our hearts.

Of course, God is tremendously generous. Even when we are rebelling, he may be gracious enough to use what we do and bring good out of it — he does it with Jonah in chapter 3. But if he does, let’s not be too flattered. God can bring good out of what Satan does; he can bring good out of our hypocrisy too. If we act sinfully and God is still gracious enough to bring good out of it, he will have done it *in spite of us*, not because of us.

God may be gracious enough to give us a second, third, fourth, or fifth chance to sort things out with him. Jonah gets a whole string of them. But practice makes perfect. Practise turning down God’s chances to repent, and you might get rather good at it. Do not do that — one day it will be too late. Instead, let’s be people who apply God’s word to ourselves, take it to heart, and put it into practice.

pft2.body:
What have you learnt recently from God’s word?

Have you put it into practice?

fishSection.header: Mercy, Judgment, and the Thing about Fish
fishSection.body:
There is one more question to look at in this chapter: what are we supposed to make of the fish? When Jonah gets swallowed by this ’great fish,‘ is that a good thing or a bad thing? God is acting in judgment — but is that judgment a ’Yes’ or a ’No’? Is it an act of condemnation or mercy? The answer is intriguing, because you can make a reasonable case both ways.[[fn:23]]

We are perhaps most used to thinking of the fish as God’s way of saving Jonah — and it certainly works out like that in the end. God uses the fish to prevent Jonah drowning and to vomit him onto dry land. Without the fish Jonah would have been dead, and chapter 2 is largely a prayer of thanksgiving. All that leans towards seeing the fish as a tool of God’s mercy.

However, it might be better to say that the fish *turns out to bring* salvation in the long run — but it is not obvious to begin with. If we did not know how the story ends, we would not think this was a good thing. Being swallowed by some enormous sea creature cannot exactly be pleasant. Indeed, the Bible does not normally associate giant sea creatures with blessings or salvation. Quite the reverse.

culturalContext.body:
Sea monsters do not have good connotations in the Bible. Our nearest modern equivalent is probably *Jaws*. Imagine watching *Jaws IX*, and one of the characters is deep underwater, running out of breath, about to drown — and suddenly Jaws appears and swallows them. This is not good news. Things were bad, but now they are terminally worse.

That is probably the first reaction the original readers would have had. The sea and giant sea creatures crop up all through the Bible — none of the references are pleasant; they are all to do with judgment, chaos, and destruction. For an ancient Israelite, Giant Sea Creatures are not a good thing, and being swallowed by one is not a happy event.

fishSection2.body:
Jonah has been stubbornly running away from God. God sends judgment against him in the form of a raging storm — but Jonah is so stubborn he would rather be thrown overboard than repent. So he is thrown headfirst into the depths of God’s judgment — and then God sends some great sea beast to swallow him. If the story had finished there, we would have said Jonah got what he deserved: someone who provoked God’s judgment and met a sticky end.

But the story does not end there. Jonah gets to taste fresh air and freedom again. And that is part of what makes Jonah distinctive — the very thing that brings judgment against him also brings salvation to him. The sea monster consumes him — and then delivers him.

In one way, this is not so unusual. Sometimes we think of salvation and judgment as opposites — but in the Bible they are not. Condemnation and salvation are opposites, and judgment is the process of deciding which one we should face. But condemnation and salvation often go together in the Bible. The good is restored precisely as the evil is abolished. Judgment is God sorting things out — saying his ’Yes’ and his ’No.’

inANutshell.body: If judgment was a coin, then salvation and condemnation would be the two sides of that one coin.

floodAndExodus.body:
Think of the Flood: Noah is delivered, but others are drowned. God overthrows evil to create a new start with Noah. The way judgment brings both salvation and condemnation is even more obvious in the Exodus — the greatest Old Testament example of salvation. The Israelites are delivered from slavery and death, and set on the road to the Promised Land. But this salvation comes precisely through God passing judgment on Egypt. You can see it in each of the Ten Plagues — the Egyptians are condemned in the plagues; Israel is saved by them. You can see it in the parting of the Red Sea. And you can see it most poignantly in the Tenth Plague, the Passover — the very same plague that judges Egypt most sharply is the one that wins Israel’s release. When God saves, he does so by overthrowing evil. God saves by judging.

So in one sense it is not unusual to find condemnation and salvation together here in Jonah — that is what judgment means: saying ’Yes’ and ’No.’ It happens all through the Bible.

But in one particular way, Jonah is very unusual. God’s condemnation and God’s salvation do normally come together — but they do not normally come together *on the same person*. In the Flood, most were condemned but Noah’s family was saved. In the Exodus, Egypt was condemned and Israel was saved. But in Jonah, Jonah is condemned *and* Jonah is saved.

This is strikingly unusual — it only happens twice in the whole Bible: once in the Old Testament, here with Jonah, and once in the New Testament. And that unusualness may help us understand another unusual passage — Matthew 12:38–41:

matthew12.body: > *Then some of the Pharisees and Scribes answered him, ‘Teacher, we want to see a sign from you.’ But he answered them, ‘An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign! And no sign will be given to it except the sign of Jonah the prophet. For just as Jonah was in the belly of the sea creature three days and three nights, so the Son of Man will be in the heart of the earth three days and three nights. The men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it — because they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and now one greater than Jonah is here.’* (Matthew 12:38–41)

signOfJonah.body:
‘Jesus never compares himself to any of the Old Testament prophets, not even the greatest ones.’ That statement is very nearly true. And so it is all the more surprising that the only exception is this passage in Matthew,[[fn:24]] where Jesus compares himself to Jonah — to a prophet who falls so far short of God’s standards. In many ways Jesus and Jonah are very, very different. But there is one crucial parallel — they both experience God’s judgment of condemnation *and* God’s judgment of salvation. Just as Jonah underwent God’s ’No’ for three days in the belly of the sea creature, so Jesus also underwent God’s ’No’ for three days in the belly of the earth. And just as Jonah experienced God’s ’Yes’ on the third day, so Jesus also experienced God’s ’Yes’ on the third day.

In all God’s purposes, his judgment involves condemnation and salvation. But in Jesus Christ, condemnation and salvation are focused together on one person. In him justice and deliverance are brought together. Because he has faced God’s ’No,‘ we can hear God’s ’Yes.’ He bears our punishment to win our peace. The cross is the place where wrath and mercy meet.

footnotes.body: Nahum is not a counter-example. His prophecy seems to have started life as a book (cf. Nahum 1:1). There is no evidence he ever went on a preaching tour to Nineveh to proclaim this message. Cf. Tremper Longman III, ‘Nahum’, p.769 in *The Minor Prophets* (Vol 2), edited by T. E. McComiskey (Grand Rapids: Baker, 1993).
For added vividness, these verses are in a very literal translation.
Literally, they ’feared with a great fear’ — contrasting Jonah’s casual rebellion.

opening.body:
When you understand the end of a story, sometimes the beginning makes much more sense. Murder-mysteries often work that way. On the first read through, there are all sorts of loose ends that do not quite add up. But when you discover who did it and what their motives were, suddenly everything else fits into place.

It is like that with the book of Jonah. Now that we know where the story ends up in chapter 4, we can better understand why it starts off the way it does in chapter 1. In chapter 4 we discover what Jonah’s motives are — what has been driving him all the way through. We find out that Jonah wants to see Nineveh destroyed. He is afraid that if he goes there and preaches, they might repent and be spared. And Jonah desperately does not want that to happen.

With that vital piece of knowledge in place, let’s rewind all the way back to the start. Let’s revisit the scenes in chapter 1 to see what else they have to show us.

scene1.body:
> *The word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai.* (1:1)

Back in Old Testament times, when people first read the book of Jonah, they must have found the start both familiar and unusual. Jonah was a familiar figure — he is a prophet who crops up in 2 Kings 14:25. But even there he is unusual. He is based in Israel during evil times. Both before his day and afterwards, God sends warnings to Israel about the judgment to come. But in 2 Kings, God’s message through Jonah is different: Jonah speaks a message of blessing and mercy for Israel, and his prophecy is quickly fulfilled — about restoring the borders of the Promised Land. So Jonah is familiar because he is already established as a true prophet — but unusual because he is a prophet of blessing during evil days, when judgment is just over the horizon.

commission.body:
Jonah himself is both familiar and unusual, and his commission is too:

> *’Go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me.’* (1:2)

This is unusual — it is the only place in the Old Testament where a prophet is called to go on a preaching tour of a pagan nation.[[fn:26]] But it is also familiar — it is the normal pattern God used to call people to proclaim his message. There are even echoes of Sodom and Gomorrah here. God acted in judgment against them because the ’outcry’ against their wickedness had reached him. Jonah’s mission to Nineveh reminds us that God is the judge of all the earth — and he will ’do right’ (Genesis 18:25). Nineveh was a leading city of its day: proud, successful, and free-spirited. Maybe it thought it could ignore God, maybe it thought it was untouchable and indestructible. It was not.

endOfWorld.body:
That is something to make us sit up and think about our own world. When we think of a proud London, a successful New York, or a free-spirited Paris, do we let them fool us into thinking they are untouchable and indestructible too? Various atrocities and disasters of recent years have dented that confidence a little. But it is easy to think that if these cities were destroyed, the world would be as good as over.

It probably felt like that in Nineveh’s day. But the world survived the eventual fall of Nineveh — and Babylon, and Athens, and Rome. It can survive the fall of London, New York, and Paris too, if God decides that should happen. This world is God’s world and it will not come to an end until he decides. In the meantime, all of our cities need to hear the same message: ‘Repent, because God will judge our wickedness.’

That is something of the message Jonah was given to preach. And already these first two verses have produced a funny mix of the familiar and the unusual. But it is Jonah’s response from verse 3 onwards where we get the strangest combination. We will see that all the more vividly if we compare Jonah’s call to a more ’normal’ prophetic call:[[fn:27]]

> *Then the word of the Lord came to him [Elijah], saying, ‘Arise and go to Zarepheth of Sidon, and stay there …’ So he arose and went to Zarepheth.* (1 Kings 17:8–10)

> *The word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai, saying, ‘Arise and go to Nineveh, the great city, and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me.’ So Jonah arose — to flee to Tarshish from the presence of the Lord.* (Jonah 1:1–3)

It is a surprising, upside-down start. Jonah does the ’arising’ alright — but in entirely the wrong direction. Which raises the question we looked at before: why does a prophet of mercy run away when he is given a message of condemnation? ’Is he too soft?’ we might think. ’Maybe he cannot cope with a message of condemnation.’

But alas, no. As we have seen in chapter 4, the truth is much worse. Jonah runs away because he is *too hard*. He does not want to give the Ninevites the slightest chance of repenting. He wants to see them destroyed. And if God says destruction is on the cards, then why rock the boat by telling them? Don’t say a word — that is the best policy.

wordThwarted.body: But this upside-down start raises another question: will God’s word be thwarted? The whole book begins with God’s word coming to Jonah, yet within two sentences it looks like that word is in jeopardy. We have read the whole book now, so we know that is not how it turns out. One of the major themes and encouragements of Jonah is that God’s word will do its job. Even in the face of stubborn rebellion by those who ought to be on God’s side, his word will still achieve his purposes. That is how powerful God’s word is; that is how powerful God is. And Scene 2 shows us that vividly.

scene2.header: Scene 2 — The Storm and the Response
scene2.body:
God is the judge of all the earth — we have heard that in verses 1–3. Now we see that he is also the King over all creation. He is the Lord who can summon heaven and earth to do his bidding, and in verse 4 he does just that:

> *The Lord hurled a great wind on the sea and there was so great a whirlwind on the sea that the ship threatened to break up. Then the sailors were afraid and each one cried out to his own god, and they hurled the ship’s cargo into the sea to lighten it for them. But Jonah had gone into the ship’s hold, lain down and fallen sound asleep. So the captain came and said to him, ‘What are you doing sound asleep? Arise and call on your god. Maybe that god will spare us a thought so that we won’t perish!’* (1:4–6)

God hurls a whirlwind onto the sea; in desperation the sailors hurl the cargo into the sea. Their efforts seem puny and pointless in the face of God’s action, but at least they are trying something. This snapshot shows us a whole range of ways people can respond to God’s judgment — fear, prayer, human effort of every kind.

lethargic.body:
And there is comatose slumber. We are not supposed to see Jonah’s sleep as peaceful trust in God’s sovereignty — Jonah is just totally desensitised.

When the captain finds Jonah, he is not impressed. He tells Jonah to ’arise’ — that is the second time Jonah has been given that command — and again Jonah does arise. But again he does not take any notice of the rest of the instruction: to pray to his god in case that god might spare them. Perhaps Jonah is aware that the Lord’s thoughts are already focused on the ship — rather too focused for comfort. Whatever he is thinking, he does not lift a finger to help the situation.

scene3.body:
Jonah seems completely lethargic. We do not hear from him until the sailors pin him down directly. They cast their lots, it falls to Jonah, and they demand answers. There is a sense of desperate panic about the rapid-fire string of questions they ask — casting around frantically for some clue about why this calamity has fallen on them. And at last Jonah responds. These are the first words we hear him speak:

> *’I am a Hebrew, and I fear the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land.’* (1:9)

You could not fault Jonah for orthodoxy. What he says is sound. It is just that it rings so hollow.

fearAndWorship.body:
He says that he *fears* the Lord. That is a normal Old Testament way of talking about what we call ’worship’. We have heard plenty about fear in chapter 1, but none of it has been shown by Jonah. He knows that the Lord is the God of heaven and he knows that God has spoken his word to him. But he does not like what God has in mind, so he quite happily turns his back on God and runs off. We have seen Jonah the rebel, Jonah the runner, and Jonah the slumberer. But we have not seen any sign of Jonah the worshipper — the one who actually fears the Lord.

The sailors know none of the truth that Jonah knows. Before verse 9 they know nothing about who the Lord is. All they get is a glimpse of his power in the storm. But that glimpse is enough to create a very healthy fear in them. To start with, it is largely a fear for their own lives — but at least that makes them aware that God is out there and drives them towards wanting to sort out things with him. Jonah has all this knowledge too, and far more besides. But despite all his knowledge, Jonah does not even show that basic fear. He shows about as much life and sensitivity as a block of wood.

Let’s think about that ’fear’ concept for a moment. If someone asked you how you felt about God, would it occur to you to use the word ’fear’ in any context whatsoever? ’Fear’ might sound negative — and certainly there are all sorts of ways of being ’afraid of God’ that are unhealthy. Perhaps that is why lots of modern translations use the word ’worship’ instead. But ’fear’ is what it originally says, and with good reason. The God we know is the awesome, fearsome, mind-blowing God who rules the whole universe. The most violent storm you have ever seen is nothing but a whisper compared to the power of our God.

pft1.body:
Do you fear God?

Yet this is also the God of tenderness, who stoops down and cares for each one of us — the God who takes on our frail flesh and blood and becomes one of us in the person of his Son, Jesus Christ. If we forget how fearfully awesome God is, we will take for granted how wonderfully precious it is to be his children.

holloFear.body:
That seems to be Jonah’s problem. He ’knows’ the truth — or at least he can spout it on demand. But it does not mean much to him, it does not shape the way he behaves. The sailors, for all their pagan ignorance, have a far better grasp of this truth than Jonah:

> *The men became fearfully afraid[[fn:28]] and they said to him, ‘What have you done?!’* (1:10)

The more the sailors learn about God, the healthier their respect for him becomes. In their case it is also terror, because they realise they do not know God, they have lived in his world as though he did not exist, and his judgment is being expressed right now against their ship. They are terrified.

scene4.body:
The sailors may be terrified, but Jonah is still as unmoved as a log. It is as if rebelling against God has made him totally insensitive. He is not interested in life; all his thoughts are focused on death — and above all, he wants the Ninevites to die. He would rather see himself drown than risk giving the Ninevites the opportunity to hear God’s word from him.

At this stage in a violent tempest, any normal person who ’feared the Lord’ would be calling out for mercy. But not Jonah. He knows mercy is linked to repentance, and repentance would involve going to Nineveh — and that would mean risking that the Ninevites might turn and be saved. Jonah does not want that risk. He would much rather die himself — and so he tells the sailors to ’hurl’ him into the sea.

ratherDie.body:
When I first heard the story of Jonah at Sunday school, I assumed God must have told Jonah the sailors needed to throw him overboard. But there is not the slightest indication of that in the text. The truth is probably much bleaker — it was Jonah’s own idea. He had no desire for either mercy or repentance. He would rather be hurled into the violence of the sea than call on the Lord of the sea for mercy.

By contrast, these rough sailors seem positively saintly. Jonah may not care about his life, or the sailors’ lives, or the Ninevites’ lives — but the sailors care about his life and do all they can to save it. But they are no match for the judgment of God, and eventually they have to recognise this. Reluctantly and fearfully, they hurl Jonah overboard.

And suddenly, just like that, the raging sea grows calm. People who know about the sea have told me that even after a storm stops, it takes a long time for the sea to settle again — once the water is churned up, it can remain rough for hours. But when God tells the sea to stop, it stops. Straight from raging storm to calm expanse. That is why the sailors sit up and take notice.

The rest of their response is remarkable (v16). They react just the way you would expect godly Israelites to react — they fear the Lord, offer sacrifices, and make vows. They respond with true worship. Jonah *says* he fears the Lord; the sailors *actually* fear the Lord. Jonah’s ’fear’ rings hollow; their fear is real and godly.

whenGodSpeaks.body:
This whole episode tells us something about what happens when God speaks. God is powerful, and his word is powerful. God is not to be trifled with, and nor is his word. That was Jonah’s mistake — he trifled with the word God had spoken to him. He thought he could ignore it, avoid it, and forget about it.

We are not Jonah; we are not prophets like Jonah. But God has spoken to us even more profoundly than he spoke to Jonah. God spoke a little piece of his message to Jonah; God has spoken supremely to us in his Son (Hebrews 1:1–3). And he will be heard.

The word ’hear’ is an odd word in English. It can mean ’listen and respond,‘ or it can mean ’listen, but without responding.’ When we talk about needing to ’hear’ God’s word, we mean ’listen and respond.’ There is an old-fashioned word that catches this better: *heed*.

God will be heeded, he will be feared — and it may be by the most unlikely people. The gospel of Jesus Christ is powerful; it can surprise us by taking hold of unlikely lives. The person who heeds that gospel word will receive salvation. The person who will not heed the word will face condemnation — just as Jonah was hurled into the sea.

Whenever we hear the word of God we are faced with a simple choice. We may respond in a thousand different ways, depending on our circumstances. But regardless of the differences, all those ways will fit into one of two stark types: we rebel, and end up with log-like slumber; or we heed, and end up with life-giving mercy. Everyone has to pick one or other. Let’s pick wisely — let’s heed.

opening.body:
Congratulations. Our double journey is complete. We have followed Jonah to Nineveh, and retraced our steps back again. We have heard God’s word to the Ninevites — and we have heard God’s word to us ourselves.

Hearing God’s word is both a tremendous opportunity and an important responsibility. I hope this book has helped you hear God’s word in Jonah — but just as importantly, I hope you *heed* it. God’s word is rich and multi-faceted, and each reader is different, so our responses will vary. But as we finish, it is important that we reflect on how God is calling each of us to respond.

fourTruths.body: Think back over the main truths we have seen, and sit with the questions they raise:

godBlock.body:
Who he is — the Sovereign Lord of everything, from whales and storms to the fall of a lot.

Do you *fear* God? Do you respect him enormously?

sinBlock.body:
How serious sin is — sin in general, and specific sins in particular.

Do you take your sin seriously?

repentanceBlock.body:
And the difference between it and regret or remorse.

Have you repented and turned to God? Are there any further things you need to repent of?

compassionBlock.body:
Even for violent, wicked people — and how totally that love is demonstrated in the death of Jesus for us.

Have you grasped how much God loves you? Have you understood his great compassion for everyone else in our world? Do you share it?

closing.body: God has spoken in Jonah. Let’s respond.

intro: These notes are written for leaders and mentors guiding others through an expository study of Jonah. Unlike a topical study, an expository study asks you to stay close to the text — to let the passage set the agenda rather than jumping too quickly to applications or cross-references. Your main job is to help people read carefully and ask good questions of what they find. That discipline is harder than it sounds, and these notes are designed to help you do it well.

Most people think they already know this story — and that is your first challenge. Jonah is Sunday school material. People in your group will arrive with a vague familiarity: prophet, storm, big fish, Nineveh, done. That prior knowledge is both an asset and an obstacle. The asset is that people are not starting from zero. The obstacle is that familiarity breeds inattention — they may stop reading carefully because they think they know what is coming. Your job in the early sessions is to create productive surprise: to help people see that the story they thought they knew is stranger, darker, and more personally confronting than they realised.

The book’s structure is its first and most important lesson. Jonah is structured as a double journey — forwards through chapters 1–4, then backwards through 4–1. This is not a gimmick; it is the author’s method. Chapter 4’s shock ending reframes everything that came before. In session 1, participants will naturally assume Jonah is a sympathetic hero who is scared. By session 4 they will know the truth is worse. Hold the mystery deliberately. Do not let early sessions resolve what the author has intentionally left open. The study is designed so that each chapter raises a question that a later chapter answers — and answers in a way that unsettles comfortable assumptions.

The withheld motive is the key to leading well. We do not learn why Jonah ran until chapter 4. Until then, every reader assumes the most sympathetic explanation — probably fear. Resist the urge to tip your hand early, even if you know the answer. The participants’ experience of reading carefully and being surprised by chapter 4 is part of what the study is trying to form in them. If you give it away in session 1, you flatten the arc. When someone in the group makes an assumption about Jonah’s motives, simply ask: What does the text actually tell us at this point?

This story holds up a mirror — and the reflection is uncomfortable. Jonah is not a villain the reader can safely keep at a distance. He is a believer who knows the right things about God, prays the right prayers, and still manages to be thoroughly wrong about the things that matter most — compassion, obedience, and the reach of God’s grace. Most churched participants will recognise him. The study is designed to make that recognition gradual and personal rather than sudden and defensive. Your role is to keep the mirror in front of the group without turning it into an accusation. The text does the indicting; your job is to create space for honest self-examination.

Work through the passage yourself before you lead. Every chapter in this study is asking people to look closely at the text — at specific words, contrasts, and ironies. You will lead that observation far better if you have done it yourself first. Don’t just read the questions; read the passage. Ask yourself what you notice. What surprises you? What does Jonah do that you recognise? What does God do that you did not expect?

The Background and Exegetical sections in each chapter’s notes are for you, not necessarily for the group. They give you the contextual and textual grounding to handle questions with confidence. You don’t need to deliver them as a lecture — but knowing them will help you stay calm when a question lands that you weren’t expecting. The Go Deeper tab covers much of the same ground for participants who want to read further; the leaders’ notes will point you to specific sections throughout.

The Go Deeper tab is a companion, not a competitor. The participant study and the Go Deeper reading cover the same chapters but at different depths and from different angles. The study questions ask people to observe the text and reflect on it personally; the Go Deeper sections offer theological and pastoral commentary. Encourage participants to read the relevant Go Deeper chapter between sessions — it enriches the discussion without replacing the experience of working through the text themselves. Each chapter’s leaders’ notes will reference specific Go Deeper sections where they are most useful.

Watch for the person who uses the whale to avoid the mirror. Jonah raises a genuinely interesting biological question — could a person survive three days inside a great fish? Some people will find that question a more comfortable place to sit than the one the text is actually asking: Do I look like Jonah? Acknowledge the question briefly (the study deliberately sets it aside, as the text itself does), and then bring the group back to what the passage is claiming. The fish appears in one verse; Jonah’s attitude to Nineveh drives the whole book.

Don’t rush the text to Jesus — but don’t avoid him either. This is an Old Testament study, and Jonah deserves to be read on its own terms first. The book has a great deal to say about God, compassion, obedience, and the reach of grace without any New Testament commentary. Let it do that work. The Making Connections sections in the Go Deeper tab draw the gospel threads explicitly when the time is right — particularly the Jonah/Jesus parallel in chapters 6 and 7. Follow those cues rather than making the connections in every session. When they land, they will land well because the ground has been properly prepared.

background.body:
The Go Deeper tab covers the essential background for this chapter well — the Nineveh and Assyria context in Jonah’s News of the World, the Israel/Judah split in Bible Background, and Tarshish’s location via footnote 6. Read those sections before leading. What follows here supplements rather than repeats them.


Jonah’s historical setting. 2 Kings 14:25 places Jonah during the reign of Jeroboam II (c.786–746 BC) — a period of unusual prosperity for the northern kingdom, but also of deep spiritual corruption. Jonah is a prophet of blessing in evil times, which makes his attitudes in chapter 4 more understandable: he has grown up in a world where Israel is fragile and Assyria is the existential threat. His hostility is not simple prejudice; it has a long and painful history behind it.


The name ’YHWH’ / ’the Lord’. The NIV renders God’s covenant name as ’the Lord’ (small capitals). The Go Deeper footnote 7 explains this. It is worth knowing so you can explain the small-caps convention quickly if anyone asks — it signals the personal name, not a title.


Why does the text not explain Jonah’s motive? This is the book’s most important structural feature, and worth flagging for yourself before leading. The author deliberately withholds Jonah’s reason for running until chapter 4. Chapter 1 is designed to make us guess — and our guesses say a great deal about our own assumptions. Most people’s first guess is fear. The truth (chapter 4) is far more troubling. Keep that in reserve; do not short-circuit the mystery in session 1.

pacing.body:
45 minutes: Open with el05 (first impressions). Take one question from Scene 1 (el08 or el10), one from Scene 2 (el13), and one from Scene 3 (el23). Close with el37 (sovereignty and current situation) as the only reflection question. Skip el28 and el30 — the sovereignty theme will emerge naturally in el37.


60 minutes: As above, but add el17 (Jonah’s confession and its irony) and el30 (what the fish tells us about God’s response to failure). You can also use el35 (the Ostrich Trick) as a reflection if the group engages well with the callout.


90 minutes: Work through all the study questions (el05, el08, el10, el13, el17, el21, el23, el28, el30) and choose two of the three reflection questions. el33 and el37 are the strongest pair — el33 is personal and forward-looking; el37 draws together the chapter’s themes. el35 works well if the group has been particularly engaged by the Jonah-asleep material.


The question to never skip, regardless of time: el23 — the sailors’ response. The irony of pagans worshipping more faithfully than the prophet is the chapter’s sharpest edge, and it feeds directly into everything that follows in the book.

keyPoints.body:
The back-to-front contrasts are the engine of the chapter. Everything else — the storm, the sleep, the confession, the fish — feeds into the central irony: the prophet behaves like a pagan, and the pagans behave like the prophet ought to. The callout Back-to-Front Contrasts (el24) lists these systematically. Make sure your group has actually sat with them, not just skimmed past them. The list is worth reading aloud and pausing after each point.


Jonah’s confession (v9) is theologically impeccable and practically absurd. He says exactly the right thing about God — the Creator of the sea — while sitting on a boat, on that sea, running from that God. The gap between knowing and living is the chapter’s central pastoral diagnosis. It will recur throughout the book. See the Go Deeper section Scene 1 — Call and Response for the full treatment, and the callout in el18 for the mirror it holds up to us.


God’s sovereignty is total and unhurried. He does not scramble to recover from Jonah’s rebellion — he uses it. The storm, the lot, the calming of the sea, the fish: all of these are God’s active provision, not reactions to a situation that got away from him. The study question el28 (list everything God controls) is the most important observation question in the chapter — it builds the foundation for everything the book will say about God. See Go Deeper God in Control for the fuller discussion.


The fish is grace, not punishment. This often surprises groups. Jonah deserved to drown; God saved him anyway — in a way that was undignified, uncomfortable, and exactly sufficient. The pattern of grace-that-doesn’t-feel-like-grace will run through the whole book (the second chance in chapter 3, the plant in chapter 4). Establish it clearly here. See Go Deeper God in Control.

pastorals.body:
The Jonah-mirror is uncomfortable early. el17 and el18 ask participants to sit with the gap between knowing truth about God and living by it — and the callout in el18 makes it personal. For a predominantly churched group this can land hard: many will recognise themselves in Jonah more than they are comfortable admitting. Do not rush past that discomfort. It is productive. But be gentle — the point is not condemnation but recognition.


The Ostrich Trick (el14, el35) may surface real situations. The reflection question el35 asks whether there is a mess someone has been ’sleeping through.’ For some people this will be abstract; for others it will be immediately, painfully concrete. Have a sense of who in your group might be in a difficult season. You do not need to draw anyone out — but be ready to follow up privately if something significant surfaces.


If seekers are present, the sovereignty material (el28, el30, el37) can raise the question of whether a God who ’sends’ storms and ’provides’ fish to manipulate events is a God worth trusting. Acknowledge that the picture of God here is demanding — he is not a gentle background presence but an active, pursuing God who will not be evaded. That is either reassuring or alarming depending on where someone stands. The question worth asking a seeker is: If this God is real, what changes? Don’t feel you need to resolve it in session 1.


’Are you running away from anything God wants you to do?’ (el33, and the Pause for Thought in el25) is the most personally exposing question in the chapter. In a first session, people may not yet feel safe enough to answer honestly in the group. That is fine. Name it as a question worth sitting with privately. Some leaders find it helpful to share a brief, honest answer from their own experience before inviting others — it gives permission for honesty without requiring it.


The sailors’ conversion (el23) can open a useful conversation about assumptions regarding who is ’in’ and ’out.’ In a churched group, some may have a Jonah-like instinct — a sense that God’s blessings belong to ’us’ rather than outsiders. The text does not let that stand. Handle it without making anyone feel personally accused; the text is doing the work.

exegetical.body:
’The word of the Lord came to Jonah’ (v1). This is the standard prophetic commissioning formula — used of Elijah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and others. Its familiarity to an Old Testament reader would set up an expectation: prophet receives word, prophet obeys, prophet goes. Verse 3 then violently breaks that expectation. The Go Deeper section Don’t Say a Word (chapter 7) develops the Elijah comparison in detail — you do not need to cover it here, but knowing it is there helps you answer the question if it comes up.


’Fled from the presence of the Lord’ (v3, repeated). The phrase is theologically loaded. Some have argued it reflects a primitive view of God as territorially limited — you could literally leave his presence by leaving Israel. More likely it is the narrator’s ironic comment: Jonah is acting as if he believes this, even though his own confession in v9 makes clear he knows better. His behaviour contradicts his theology — which is precisely the point.


The casting of lots (v7). Lot-casting was a common ancient method for determining divine will — not mere gambling but an appeal to divine guidance. The Go Deeper footnote 7 does not cover this, but participants may ask. The point is that God’s direction of the lot (implied by v7’s outcome) shows his control extending even into what looks like chance. Proverbs 16:33 makes this explicit: ‘The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the Lord.’


’Pick me up and throw me into the sea’ (v12). Notice what Jonah does not say. He does not pray. He does not repent. He does not offer to turn around and go to Nineveh. His solution is self-removal, not course-correction. The sample answer to el21 makes this point well. It is worth distinguishing clearly between taking responsibility (which Jonah does) and repentance (which he does not). That distinction will become critical in chapter 2.


The sailors’ prayer (v14). This is a remarkable moment. Before throwing Jonah overboard, the sailors pray — to the Lord, by name — asking not to be held guilty for an innocent man’s blood. They are applying a theology of bloodguilt they have apparently absorbed from Jonah’s testimony, and they are praying to the right God, correctly. Pagan sailors are doing better theology than the prophet. The study question el23 is the place to draw this out.


’The Lord provided a great fish’ (v17). The Hebrew verb (manah) means to appoint, assign, or ordain. It is used three more times in the book (the plant, the worm, the scorching wind — all in chapter 4). The repetition is deliberate: God is the one who assigns and provides throughout. Everything that happens to Jonah — good or bad, comfortable or not — is the Lord’s provision. See Go Deeper God in Control.

watch.body:
👁️ Watch for the person who wants to spend time on the whale. ’Was it really a whale? Could a person survive three days inside a fish?’ These are the questions people who have only half-heard Jonah tend to bring. Acknowledge them briefly — the Go Deeper footnote 1 notes that the text simply says ’great fish’ without specifying species, and the biological question is deliberately set aside. Then move on. The book itself is not interested in the biology; neither should your group be. If someone is genuinely stuck on it, the question worth asking them is: If you knew for certain it happened exactly as described, what would that change for you?


👁️ Watch for the person who is already in chapter 4 without knowing it. Some churched participants will arrive knowing that Jonah ends badly — that he is angry God spared Nineveh. If they import that knowledge into chapter 1, it short-circuits the author’s carefully constructed mystery. The study is designed to let chapter 4 land as a surprise. If this comes up, you can acknowledge it honestly (‘Yes, we’ll get there’) and ask the group to sit with chapter 1 on its own terms for now: What do we know at this point, and what are we guessing?


👁️ Watch for the person who over-identifies with Jonah’s sympathy for Israel. In a group with any awareness of contemporary geopolitics, the Nineveh/Israel dynamic can become a proxy for modern conflicts. Jonah’s hostility to Nineveh is understandable, but the book does not endorse it. If this surface, bring the group back to the text: What does God think of Nineveh in this chapter? The answer — he sends his prophet to warn them — is the datum the discussion needs to return to.

goDeeperRefs.body:
The following sections of the Go Deeper tab are directly relevant to this chapter’s study questions and themes. Point participants to these for further reading between sessions.


  • Jonah’s News of the World — Nineveh and Assyria background; why the call was so daunting. Supplements el02 and el08.
  • Bible Background — The Israel/Judah split; Jonah’s location in the north. Supplements el02.
  • Scene 1 — Call and Response — The geography of Jonah’s flight; Tarshish; the double ’flee from the Lord.’ Supplements el08 and el10.
  • Scene 2 — Storm and Response — The storm’s severity; Jonah’s sleep as desensitisation; the captain’s ironic rebuke. Supplements el13.
  • Scene 3 — Crunch Time — Jonah’s confession and its absurdity; the sailors’ growing theology; the back-to-front contrasts. Supplements el17, el21, el23.
  • God in Control — God’s sovereignty throughout chapter 1; the fish as grace; the verb provided. Supplements el28 and el30, and the reflection question el37.

background.body:
The Go Deeper tab covers the theological content of this chapter well across Sorrow Realises There is a Problem, Calling to God for Help, The God who Saves, Jonah’s Thankfulness, and Knowing the Truth and Living It. Read those sections before leading. One thing the Go Deeper tab does not develop, which is worth knowing as a leader:


Jonah’s prayer is saturated with the Psalms. Almost every phrase in chapter 2 echoes a psalm of lament — the ’depths of the grave’ (Psalm 88), crying from deep waters (Psalm 69), the ’holy temple’ as the focus of prayer (Psalm 5, 18, 28), the ’pit’ (Psalm 30). Jonah is not composing original poetry; he is praying the prayer book of Israel. This matters for two reasons. First, it shows Jonah knows his Bible extremely well — his theology is sound and his liturgical instincts are good. Second, it raises the uncomfortable question (which chapters 4 and 6 will force us to face) of whether someone can pray all the right words and still not have fully repented. Knowing this in advance will help you hold the chapter’s tension without resolving it prematurely.


The fish is not yet over. A common assumption is that the fish = punishment and vomiting onto dry land = rescue. The Go Deeper chapter 6 section Mercy, Judgment, and the Thing about Fish unpacks why this is more complicated. You do not need to develop it here — it belongs to the second journey through the book. But do not allow the group to treat v10 as a clean happy ending. It is a rescue — but it deposits Jonah back at square one, with the call to Nineveh still unanswered.

pacing.body:
45 minutes: Open with el04 (tone of the prayer). Take el07 (the imagery of descent) and el10 (the hinge of v4) from the first section, then el21 (knowing vs living) as the central question. Close with el35 as the only reflection question — it is the most practically focused. Skip el13, el15, el19, el24, el27.


60 minutes: As above, adding el13 (why it took extremity to make Jonah pray) and el27 (God’s response to the returning failure). These two questions bracket the prayer’s movement well and give you the chapter’s pastoral arc without overloading the session. Use el33 (‘yet’) as a second reflection if time allows.


90 minutes: Work through el04, el07, el10, el13, el15, el19, el21, el24, el27, then use all three reflection questions. el31 (prayer as first response) and el33 (‘yet’) work well back to back — el31 is practical, el33 is more personal and raw. End with el35 as the concrete take-home.


The question to never skip: el10 — the hinge of v4. The word ’yet’ is the theological and pastoral centre of the chapter. Every other question builds toward or flows from it. If you only have time for one question from this chapter, make it this one.

keyPoints.body:
The imagery of descent in vv3–6 is the chapter’s foundation. Jonah does not understate his situation — he is at the absolute limit of human helplessness. The pile-up of images (hurled into the deep, currents, breakers, seaweed, roots of the mountains, earth barred in forever) is designed to make the reader feel the impossibility. The point is simple but crucial: when rescue comes from here, it can only be God. There is nothing left for human effort to contribute. See Go Deeper Sorrow Realises There is a Problem. Study question el07 is where the group observes this — give it time.


V4 is the pivot of the whole prayer — and of the whole chapter. ’I have been banished from your sight; yet I will look again towards your holy temple.’ The ’yet’ holds together honest acknowledgment of felt reality with deliberate trust. This is the model of lament prayer in the Psalms, and Jonah is drawing on it consciously. Help the group see that faith here is not the absence of distress — it is the decision to orient toward God in the middle of it. Study question el10 develops this. See Go Deeper Calling to God for Help.


V8’s irony about idols is the chapter’s sharpest edge — and the leader needs to hold it carefully. In chapter 1, it was the sailors who turned from idols to the Lord; it was Jonah who was effectively running from God. Now Jonah denounces idol-clingers. The irony is unmistakeable — and yet the chapter does not make Jonah aware of it. He is preaching a sermon he has just lived from the wrong side. Study question el19 is where this lands. Do not over-press it here — let the group feel the irony rather than spelling it out for them. The fuller treatment belongs to chapters 5 and 6 of the study.


’Salvation comes from the Lord’ (v9) is known truth becoming lived truth — partially. This is the chapter’s high note and its most important pastoral bridge. Jonah knew this before he boarded the ship. What has changed is that he has now tasted it. But the book will show us — in chapter 4 — that tasting grace is not the same as being transformed by it. Keep the question open: will Jonah live differently? Study question el21 and reflection question el35 carry this. See Go Deeper Knowing the Truth and Living It and Making Connections.


God’s response in v10 is immediate, complete, and generous — and sets up everything that follows. Jonah prays with thankfulness and God acts without delay. There is no probationary period. The rescue is real. But v10 deposits Jonah on a beach with the same unanswered call still in front of him. The generosity of God’s rescue is the foundation for what chapter 3 will demand of Jonah — and, the book will argue, what ought to flow naturally from having received such grace. Study question el27 is the place to establish this. See Go Deeper Jonah’s Thankfulness.

pastorals.body:
The key pastoral tension in this chapter is the gap between sounding repentant and being repentant. Jonah’s prayer sounds good. If you came to it without chapter 4, you would probably assume he was genuinely transformed. Most churched participants will want to read it charitably. That instinct is not wrong — the text does not condemn Jonah’s prayer. But it also leaves something unsaid. As leader, hold this gently: the prayer is real, the thankfulness is real, the rescue is real — and something is still incomplete. You do not need to name that gap explicitly in session 2; the study will bring it into focus later. But do not let the group close the chapter feeling everything is resolved.


The question of prayer as last resort (el13, el31) will resonate with almost everyone in a churched group — and it may produce either honest self-recognition or defensive rationalisation. The honest self-recognition is what you are after. If someone shares that they have been trying to manage a situation without praying about it, resist the urge to immediately reassure them. Let the recognition do its work first. The Pause for Thought in el14 (‘Are you facing trouble at the moment? Have you called out to God for help?’) can be used as a moment of genuine quiet if the group is willing.


The thankfulness question (el24, el25) can surface real pain. Some people find it genuinely difficult to be thankful because their circumstances feel too hard, or because past experience has taught them that things they hoped for did not materialise. Do not force gratitude. The point the text is making is not ’try harder to be grateful’ but ’Jonah could see the grace in his uncomfortable rescue.’ The question worth asking is not ’are you grateful?’ but ’can you see what God has actually done?’


The idol question (el19) is the most personally exposing in the chapter. The deeper question — ‘what are the worthless idols in your own life?’ — invites real honesty about what people actually trust in and cling to. In a churched group this is likely to produce good answers (control, reputation, comfort, financial security, relationships). Do not rush the silence that may follow. If someone names something specific and courageous, honour it without turning it into a teaching moment.


If seekers are present, the ’depths of the grave’ language in vv2–6 and the concept of a God who hears from impossible places may be the most accessible entry point in the chapter. The question el15 (‘what kind of situations is it worth praying in?’) invites anyone — churched or not — to consider whether the reach of this God is wider than they assumed. Handle it as an open question, not a conclusion.

exegetical.body:
’From inside the fish Jonah prayed’ (v1). The chapter opens by locating the prayer spatially — inside the fish, not after being deposited on land. This matters: Jonah does not wait for rescue before giving thanks. He prays from within the predicament. This is the same pattern as the Psalms of lament, where trust is expressed before resolution arrives. It is worth noting that the prayer is entirely in the past tense for the rescue (‘he answered me’, ‘you listened’, ‘you brought my life up from the pit’) — Jonah is speaking as though the rescue has already happened, while he is still inside the fish. This is an act of faith, not a report of events.


’The depths of the grave’ (v2, Sheol). The NIV’s ’depths of the grave’ translates Sheol — the Hebrew word for the realm of the dead, the underworld. It is not simply a metaphor for danger; it is the place from which, in Old Testament thought, there is no return. Jonah is saying he called from the land of the dead. This makes the rescue all the more theologically loaded — it is a kind of resurrection. The deeper question in el07 invites participants to sit with this.


’Yet I will look again towards your holy temple’ (v4). The ’holy temple’ almost certainly refers to the Jerusalem temple — the place where God’s presence dwelt and where prayer was directed (cf. 1 Kings 8:29–30, Daniel 6:10). Jonah is oriented toward the centre of Israel’s worship even from the bottom of the sea. The significance is not just geographical but theological: he is consciously aligning himself with the community of God’s people and their mode of approach to God, even in extremis.


’Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit God’s love’ (v8). The Hebrew behind ’God’s love’ here is hesed — the covenant-love, the steadfast faithfulness of God to his people. The NIV renders it well. Clinging to idols is not just a religious mistake; it is a forfeiture of the most precious thing available. The irony (noted in the keypoints above) is that Jonah has been behaving in a way that looks rather like clinging to his own preferences over God’s call. He does not seem to notice. That blindness is the subject of the second journey through the book.


’Salvation comes from the Lord’ (v9). This is the theological summit of the prayer and one of the Bible’s most compact summaries of the gospel. Yeshua — salvation — is from YHWH. It is also, of course, the root from which the name ’Jesus’ derives (Yeshua, ‘God saves’). You do not need to make this connection explicitly unless your group is ready for it — but it is there, and it is not accidental.


’The Lord commanded the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land’ (v10). The verb ’commanded’ is significant — the same God who commanded the storm now commands the fish. Everything in the narrative is under his authority. The undignified verb ’vomited’ is probably deliberate: it is not a graceful exit, and it underlines that what is happening is purely God’s action rather than any natural sequence. Jonah does not swim out; he is expelled. See Go Deeper Jonah’s Thankfulness and the Mercy, Judgment, and the Thing about Fish section in chapter 6 of the Go Deeper tab for the fuller theological treatment of the fish’s ambiguous role.

watch.body:
👁️ Watch for the person who closes this chapter feeling Jonah is now fixed. The prayer is beautiful, the rescue is real, and v10 feels like a resolution. But the study is designed to keep the question open. If someone says ’so Jonah finally repented and everything was fine,‘ hold it gently: ‘That’s what it looks like — we’ll see what chapter 3 shows us.’ You are planting a seed of curiosity, not correcting a mistake. Chapter 4 will do the work you are guarding space for.


👁️ Watch for the person who identifies strongly with Jonah’s prayer and draws too much comfort from it too quickly. This can happen with people who are in genuine distress — they hear Jonah’s language of desperation and rescue and immediately apply it to themselves, which is not wrong. But if they skip the questions the text is also asking — about what brought Jonah here, about what is still incomplete — they may miss the chapter’s harder pastoral challenge. Let the comfort land, then gently ask: ‘Is there anything in Jonah’s situation you also recognise from the wrong side — the running, the sleeping, the not-praying?’


👁️ Watch for the idol question (el19) becoming too abstract. ’What idols do you cling to?’ is easy to answer with safe, generic answers (money, success, busyness). The more useful answers are specific and personal. If the conversation stays too comfortable, you might ask: ‘What is the thing you would find hardest to give up — or give over to God?’ That question tends to land closer to the truth.

goDeeperRefs.body:
The following sections of the Go Deeper tab are directly relevant to this chapter’s study questions and themes.


  • Sorrow Realises There is a Problem — the descent imagery of vv3–6; helplessness as the precondition for rescue. Supplements el07.
  • Calling to God for Help — the ’no atheists in foxholes’ dynamic; God’s reach from impossible places. Supplements el13 and el15.
  • The God who Saves — God hears from the depths of the grave; the pivot of v4. Supplements el10 and el15.
  • Jonah’s Thankfulness — grace in uncomfortable form; God’s top priority not a trouble-free life; the two-stage rescue. Supplements el24 and el27.
  • Knowing the Truth and Living It — the gap between known truth and lived truth; the open question of whether Jonah will change. Supplements el21 and el35.
  • Making Connections — the Matthew 7 bridge from knowing to building. Supplements el28 and el35.
  • Mercy, Judgment, and the Thing about Fish (Go Deeper chapter 6) — the fish as both judgment and mercy; the Jonah/Jesus parallel. Background for leaders; introduce to participants who want to go further.

background.body:
The Go Deeper tab covers the content of this chapter across Scene 1 — Jonah’s Second Chance, Scene 2 — A Three-Day-Visit City, Scene 3 — Nineveh Takes its Second Chance, and Scene 4 — God Has Compassion on Nineveh. Read those sections before leading. Two things worth knowing as a leader that Go Deeper does not develop at length:


Chapter 3 is the pivot — and it has a false-resolution problem. Structurally, chapter 3 is where everything appears to go right: Jonah obeys, Nineveh repents, God relents. A reader stopping here would feel satisfied. But the book does not stop here — and that apparent resolution is exactly what makes chapter 4 so disorienting. As you lead session 3, you are guiding people toward a feeling of satisfaction that chapter 4 is about to demolish. Hold the question gently but deliberately: Is Jonah actually glad about any of this? The text deliberately does not say. That silence is the author’s signal that something is unresolved.


The Ninevites’ repentance and the question of its depth. The text presents the Ninevites’ response as genuine, and God treats it as such (v10). But later Assyrian history suggests Nineveh did not remain repentant — the city was eventually destroyed in 612 BC, which Nahum’s prophecy addresses. You do not need to bring this up unless it arises, but it is worth knowing: the text is not claiming permanent transformation, only genuine response. The focus is on what God does with honest repentance when he sees it, not on whether it lasted.

pacing.body:
45 minutes: Open with el05 (surprises). Take el08 (the repeated commission) and el11 (obedience without sorted feelings) from Scene 1 — these two together establish the chapter’s most important question about Jonah. Then el21 (the three stages of repentance) and el26 (what God’s compassion tells us about his character). Close with el37 (who have you written off?) as the only reflection question.


60 minutes: As above, adding el18 (‘believed God’ — what three things does that imply?) and el29 (the limits of God’s willingness to forgive). This gives you full coverage of the Nineveh repentance and God’s response. Use el35 (have you been through the three stages?) as a second reflection if time allows — it is the most personally searching question in the chapter.


90 minutes: Work through all eight study questions (el05, el08, el11, el14, el18, el21, el26, el29) and use all three reflection questions. In a 90-minute session, the Pause for Thought in el23 (‘Have you recognised your own sin?’) deserves genuine quiet time rather than being skimmed past — consider reading it aloud and allowing 60 seconds of silence before moving to el35.


The question to never skip: el21 — the three-stage repentance model. This is the most directly evangelistic question in the chapter. Some in your group may not have gone through this process themselves, and the question is designed to surface that without being confrontational. It also underpins el35, which cannot land well if el21 has been skipped.

keyPoints.body:
The repeated commission is the chapter’s first and most important theological statement. Verses 1–2 are almost word-for-word identical to 1:1–2. God comes back with the same call, to the same prophet, with no modification. He has not found a more willing messenger, has not lowered his standards, has not given Jonah an easier assignment. This is what ’God of the second chance’ actually means: not a God who softens his demands when we fail, but a God who keeps coming back. El08 is where the group observes this — make sure they actually read 1:1–2 alongside 3:1–2. The contrast of what changes (Jonah’s response) and what doesn’t (the commission) is the point. See Go Deeper Scene 1 — Jonah’s Second Chance.


Jonah obeys — but his feelings are not settled, and the text knows it. V3 describes behaviour, not inner state. The study question el11 raises this explicitly. It is worth dwelling on: genuine obedience does not always require sorted feelings first. Jonah is a case study in doing the right thing for complex, possibly mixed motives — and God uses him anyway. The leader needs to hold this carefully: it is not a license for joyless, grudging compliance as a permanent state, but it is a realistic picture of how obedience often begins. The feelings may follow the action rather than precede it. See Go Deeper Scene 1 — Jonah’s Second Chance.


’The Ninevites believed God’ — and this phrase carries three simultaneous meanings. El18 unpacks this: they believe in the God Jonah proclaims, they believe God is right to be angry with them, and they believe God means what he says. All three together constitute genuine faith rather than mere acknowledgment. The third element — believing God means what he says — is what drives the urgency of their response. People who genuinely believe God’s word is true and that consequences are real act differently from those who treat religious warnings as optional. See Go Deeper Scene 3 — Nineveh Takes its Second Chance.


The three-stage repentance model (sin, sorrow, sackcloth) is the most directly evangelistic material in the chapter. The callout el22 makes it explicit: recognising sin, caring that it is wrong, and responding to God — with the specific claim that the required response is trust in Jesus. Some in your group may not have gone through this process. El35 is designed to surface that honestly and personally. Handle the evangelistic weight of this section without either avoiding it or turning the session into an altar call. The text is doing the inviting; your job is to make sure people have genuinely heard it. See Go Deeper Scene 3 — Nineveh Takes its Second Chance and Making Connections.


God’s compassion in v10 is costly, not casual. The Making Connections callout (el30) draws Romans 3:25–26 explicitly: God can have compassion on Nineveh because Jesus will bear what Nineveh deserves. This is the strongest gospel bridge in the study so far, and it is worth handling with care. The point is not just that God is kind — it is that his forgiveness has a cost, and that cost is what makes the ’who knows?’ of the king’s prayer come back as ’yes.’ See Go Deeper Scene 4 — God Has Compassion on Nineveh and Making Connections.

pastorals.body:
The three-stage repentance model (el21, el35) may land differently than you expect. For some churched participants, the three stages will be familiar ground — perhaps too familiar, talked through so often that the personal application has been lost. For others, particularly those who came to faith recently or are still exploring, one or more stages may feel genuinely unresolved. And for a small number, the question ’have you been through this process?’ may open something significant and tender. Read the room before pushing for personal answers to el35 in the group. You may want to frame it as something to sit with privately rather than answer aloud.


’Obedience without sorted feelings’ (el11) is both liberating and potentially dangerous. For people who have been waiting until they feel ready or spiritually adequate before obeying, the observation that Jonah went without his feelings being right is freeing. But handle its shadow side: it can also be used to excuse permanently joyless, resentful compliance — which is exactly where Jonah ends up in chapter 4. The point is that feelings need not precede the first step of obedience; it is not a license to stay resentful indefinitely.


The ’who have you written off?’ question (el37) is this chapter’s most personally exposing reflection. Most churched people have a private category of ’unreachable’ — someone too hardened, too hostile, too lost in a pattern of behaviour. This question is designed to surface that honestly. If it does, resist the urge to immediately reassure (‘of course God can reach them!’). Let the person name it. Then the text speaks: Nineveh was as unreachable as it gets, and God reached it through one reluctant sentence. The question moves to prayer: are you willing to pray for this person’s repentance and mean it? That is where el37’s deeper question takes it.


If seekers are present, this is the chapter most likely to prompt genuine personal response — particularly through el23 (‘Have you recognised your own sin? Do you care that it is wrong? Have you turned to God for forgiveness?’) and the Making Connections callout. Do not rush past these. If someone seems to be engaging with them seriously, consider a brief conversation after the session rather than putting them on the spot in the group.


The Romans 3 connection (el30) may open the question of how forgiveness works. Some will ask: if God simply forgives people who repent, is that not just letting people off? The Making Connections callout addresses this directly — forgiveness is not free for God, it is satisfied at cost — but if the question surfaces, it is worth spending time on. The question in el29’s deeper section (Romans 3:25–26) is designed exactly for this moment. See also Go Deeper Making Connections in chapter 3 for fuller treatment including the Heine quote.

exegetical.body:
’The word of the Lord came to Jonah a second time’ (v1). The phrase ’a second time’ is the narrator’s quiet understatement of everything that has happened in chapters 1 and 2. No explanation is offered; no assessment of Jonah’s spiritual state is given. God simply recommissions him. The economy of the text is part of its point: God does not make a speech about what happened, does not require Jonah to demonstrate his repentance first. He gives the second chance before it has been earned.


’Proclaim to it the message I give you’ (v2). This wording is slightly different from 1:2, where Jonah was told to ’preach against it.’ Here God says ’the message I give you’ — which has led some commentators to suggest the content of the proclamation was not entirely Jonah’s composition but divinely supplied. This may explain why such a minimal message had such extraordinary effect: it was God’s word, not Jonah’s rhetoric, that did the work. The study question el14 probes this implicitly — what is implicit in the act of God sending a message at all, even through an unwilling messenger.


’Jonah obeyed’ (v3) — what kind of obedience? The Hebrew is straightforward: he obeyed the word of the Lord and went. The text does not qualify this. But the group will be reading this alongside chapter 4, where Jonah’s fury at the outcome is unambiguous. Leaders should resist reading chapter 4 back into v3 prematurely, while being willing to hold the question open: what kind of obedience was this? The study is designed to let that question ripen into chapter 4.


’A visit required three days’ (v3). This phrase has generated debate — was Nineveh literally large enough to require three days to traverse? Archaeology suggests the city proper was large but probably not three-days-walk large. The phrase likely refers to the time required to fully complete a diplomatic or prophetic mission throughout the city — including time for the message to travel, responses to be received, and the whole circuit to be completed. Jonah begins on day one and the response has clearly spread city-wide by v5.


’Forty more days and Nineveh will be destroyed’ (v4). This is eight words in the NIV; five words in the Hebrew. It is worth reading the Hebrew brevity alongside the Elijah comparison in Go Deeper chapter 7 — Jonah’s sermon is the polar opposite of Elijah’s elaborate confrontation with the prophets of Baal. There is no call to repent, no explanation of who God is, no offer of mercy. The word ’destroyed’ (nehpakat) is the same verb used for the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah — the Ninevites would have understood the register.


’The Ninevites believed God’ (v5) vs ’believed in God.’ The distinction the sample answer in el18 makes is exegetically sound. ’Believed God’ (he’eminu be’elohim) has the sense of trusting what God says — taking his word as reliable and acting accordingly. It is the same construction used of Abraham in Genesis 15:6. The Ninevites’ response has the structure of genuine faith: they hear, they believe, they act.


’Who knows? God may yet relent’ (v9). The king’s uncertainty is not a failure of faith but an honest theological position — he knows enough to repent but not enough to guarantee the outcome. This is worth noting: genuine repentance does not require certainty that it will ’work.’ The king acts without a guarantee. God’s actual response (v10) shows that the ’who knows?’ is answered with a resounding ’yes’ — but the king could not have known that in advance. His willingness to act without certainty is itself part of what genuine faith looks like. El21’s deeper question and el35’s deeper question both push toward this point.


’He had compassion and did not bring upon them the destruction he had threatened’ (v10). The Hebrew word for ’had compassion’ is nacham — sometimes translated ’relented’ or ’repented’ (as in some older translations). It does not mean God changed his mind arbitrarily. See the Jeremiah 18:7–8 passage in el26’s deeper question, which makes the conditional structure of God’s judgments explicit. God’s character is consistent; what changed was Nineveh’s behaviour. El26 is designed to surface this carefully.

watch.body:
👁️ Watch for the group closing chapter 3 feeling that everything is now resolved. It is the natural response: Jonah obeyed, Nineveh repented, God relented — happy ending.
