  • Repentance: Is there an area of my life where I have settled for remorse rather than repentance — feeling bad without actually turning?
  • Compassion: What is the name of the person I am not yet praying for, but should be? And what is one specific step I will take this week?

watch.body:
👁️ Watch for session 8 feeling anticlimactic after a strong session 7. If session 7 ended well, participants may arrive at session 8 feeling the study is already complete — and they are right, in a sense. Do not force momentum that is not there. Let the session be quieter and more consolidating than the earlier sessions. The four questions are not designed to create new intensity; they are designed to give what was said in session 7 a place to settle and become concrete. A quieter session 8 after a strong session 7 is a success.


👁️ Watch for el08 (repentance) opening something significant for someone who has been holding back. Final sessions sometimes release what earlier sessions have held in reserve. Someone who has been intellectually engaged but personally guarded through sessions 1–7 may find that the direct, plain question of el08 — ‘is there anything stopping you from turning to God now?’ — reaches them in a way that seven sessions of close observation did not. Be ready for that. Do not be so focused on moving through the four questions that you miss a person who is genuinely responding to the gospel.


👁️ Watch for el11’s concrete step being promised but not planned. ’I will pray for my colleague’ is a genuine intention that evaporates by Thursday without a specific plan. Before closing, ask the group: When specifically? In what context? Is there anything that would make it more likely to happen? The difference between a good intention and an actual action is usually a specific time and a specific trigger. Help the group build that specificity into their answer before they leave.


👁️ Watch the time carefully so the closing is never rushed. Session 8 has four questions and no other content. It should be the most spacious session in the study. If you find yourself rushing toward the end, you have allocated the time poorly. The closing — el12 read aloud, then prayer — is the one moment in the whole study that must not feel hurried. Five words and then silence: ’God has spoken in Jonah. Let’s respond.’

goDeeperRefs.body:
Session 8 draws on the whole study rather than specific new sections. The most useful Go Deeper references for this session are consolidating rather than new material:


  • God in Control (Go Deeper chapter 1) and When God Speaks… (Go Deeper chapter 7) — God’s sovereignty across the whole book. Background for Truth 1.
  • Feeling the Wrong Thing (Go Deeper chapter 4) and Jonah’s Blindness (Go Deeper chapter 6) — the specific shape of Jonah’s sin. Background for Truth 2.
  • Repentance Again (Go Deeper chapter 5) and A Whole New Leaf? (Go Deeper chapter 6) — repentance vs remorse; the Ninevites as the positive model. Background for Truth 3.
  • Learning to Be Passionate for Our World (Go Deeper chapter 4) — the three practical responses to God’s compassion; supporting proclamation; praying specifically. Background for Truth 4 and el11.
  • Making Connections (Go Deeper chapter 3 and 4) — the cost of forgiveness; the cross as the ground of God’s compassion. Background for el11’s deeper question.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Travelling with Jonah began as a series of sermons on Jonah. Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
Travelling with Jonah is available in both paperback and free e-book formats from www.beaconlight.co.uk.

studyAiData.aiMentorPrompt: You are a warm, thoughtful mentor for a Bible study called ’Travelling with Jonah: a double journey’ — exploring the Old Testament book of Jonah. The study journeys through Jonah twice: first forwards (chapters 1–4), then backwards (chapters 4–1). Key themes include God’s sovereignty, repentance vs remorse, the difference between worldly and godly sorrow, the extraordinary compassion of God, and what it means to truly fear the Lord. The study draws connections between Jonah and the death and resurrection of Jesus.
